<sup>3</sup>For we too were once foolish, disobedient, misled, enslaved to various passions and desires, spending our lives in evil and envy, hateful and hating one another. <sup>4</sup>But when the kindness of God our Savior and his love for humanity appeared, <sup>5</sup>he saved us not because of any righteous deeds we did but because of his mercy, through the washing of new birth and the renewing of the Holy Spirit, <sup>6</sup>whom he poured out on us in full measure through Jesus Christ our Savior. <sup>7</sup>And so, since we have been justified by his grace, we become heirs with the confident expectation of eternal life. <sup>8</sup>This saying is trustworthy, and I want you to insist on these things, so that those who have believed in God may be careful to engage in good works. These things are good and beneficial for all people.

elements.question: TIME AND THE GOSPEL: Looking at Titus 2:11-14 and Titus 3:3-8, how does the gospel relate to the past, the present, and the future? What has God done, what is he doing, and what will he do?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Try to identify what each passage says about the past (what God has done), the present (how we live now), and the future (what we are waiting for)…
elements.sampleAnswer:
These two passages together give the gospel a full three-dimensional shape across time:

The past: God's grace has appeared in the person of Jesus Christ, who gave himself to set us free from lawlessness (Titus 2:11, 14). We were once foolish, disobedient, and enslaved to desires — but God's kindness and love appeared (Titus 3:3-4). He saved us — not because of any righteous deeds we did but because of his mercy — through the washing of new birth and the renewing of the Holy Spirit (Titus 3:5). These are completed, historical acts.

The present: The gospel trains us to reject godless ways and to live self-controlled, upright, and godly lives in the present age (Titus 2:12). Those who believe are to be careful to engage in good works (Titus 3:8). The gospel is not only for the moment of conversion — it shapes daily life.

The future: We wait for the glorious appearing of our great God and Saviour, Jesus Christ (Titus 2:13). We have been justified by grace and become heirs with the confident expectation of eternal life (Titus 3:7). The gospel gives us a future hope that reframes how we live in the present.
elements.questionHint: Look for verbs in different tenses: what 'has appeared', what 'trains us', what we 'wait for'. The gospel spans all of time — past act, present transformation, future hope. Can you map each passage onto that framework?

elements.text: Additional Passages (if time allows)

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>Such prayer for all is good and welcomed before God our Savior, <sup>4</sup>since he wants all people to be saved and to come to a knowledge of the truth. <sup>5</sup>For there is one God and one intermediary between God and humanity, Christ Jesus, himself human, <sup>6</sup>who gave himself as a ransom for all, revealing God's purpose at his appointed time. <sup>7</sup>For this I was appointed a preacher and apostle — I am telling the truth; I am not lying — and a teacher of the Gentiles in faith and truth.

elements.question: What additional light does 1 Timothy 2:3-7 shed on the gospel? What does it add to what you have already discovered about the focus, foundation, and reach of the gospel?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at what this passage says about God's desire, about who Jesus is and what he has done, and about the scope of the gospel…
elements.sampleAnswer:
1 Timothy 2:3-7 adds several important dimensions:
The reach of the gospel: God wants all people to be saved and to come to a knowledge of the truth (v. 4). The gospel is not for a select group — its scope is universal.
The uniqueness of the gospel's mediator: There is one God and one intermediary between God and humanity — Christ Jesus, himself human (v. 5). The gospel is exclusive in its mediator even as it is universal in its invitation.
The ransom at the heart of the gospel: Christ gave himself as a ransom for all (v. 6). This is the price of redemption — Jesus did not merely teach or inspire; he gave himself.
The appointed timing of the gospel: This was revealed at God's appointed time (v. 6) — the gospel is not an accident of history but the outworking of God's sovereign purpose.
elements.questionHint: Compare this passage with 2 Tim 1:8-14. What does 1 Tim 2:3-7 add that was not as clearly stated before? Pay attention to the word 'ransom' in v. 6 — what does that word suggest about what the gospel cost?

elements.text: 6.3.2 Pulling Our Findings Together
Use the questions below to summarise what you have discovered about the gospel from the Pastoral Epistles. Draw on all the passages you have studied.

elements.question: WHAT is the gospel? Summarise its content in your own words.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Drawing on everything you have studied — what is the gospel? Try to write a clear, complete summary in 3-5 sentences.
elements.sampleAnswer: The gospel is the good news of what God has done in Jesus Christ to save sinners. God planned this in eternity — granting grace to us in Christ before time began — and revealed it in history through the appearing of Jesus, who abolished death and brought life and immortality to light. Christ gave himself as a ransom, dying in the place of sinners, and rising again. Through repentance and faith in Christ, those who were once enslaved to sin and facing death are saved, called with a holy calling, justified by God's grace, given new life by the Holy Spirit, and made heirs of eternal life. The gospel produces a life of godliness now, as those who have received it wait for the return of Christ.
elements.questionHint: Try to include: what God has done in Christ, on what basis, for whom, and to what end. A good summary of the gospel covers the problem, the solution, and the hope it creates.

elements.question: WHO is involved in the gospel? Name the key persons and describe their role.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about God the Father, Jesus Christ, the Holy Spirit, Paul, Timothy, and 'all people' — what role does each play in the gospel?
elements.sampleAnswer:
God the Father is the originator of the gospel — it flows from his own purpose and grace (2 Tim 1:9), his desire that all people be saved (1 Tim 2:4), and his kindness and love (Titus 3:4).
Jesus Christ is the mediator and the content of the gospel — the one whose appearing revealed God's grace (Titus 2:11), who abolished death and brought life to light (2 Tim 1:10), who gave himself as a ransom (1 Tim 2:6), and who will appear again in glory (Titus 2:13).
The Holy Spirit applies the gospel — through the washing of new birth and renewal (Titus 3:5) and by indwelling believers to guard the good deposit (2 Tim 1:14).
Paul is an appointed guardian and herald of the gospel — a preacher, apostle, and teacher (2 Tim 1:11; 1 Tim 2:7).
Timothy (and by extension all gospel workers) is called to guard, hold to, and pass on the gospel.
All people are the intended recipients of the gospel's offer (1 Tim 2:4).
elements.questionHint: Work through the Trinitarian shape of the gospel first (Father, Son, Spirit), then add the human participants. What does each person contribute to the gospel — and what does that tell you about the nature of God?

elements.question: WHY is there a gospel at all? What makes it necessary?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the human condition as the Pastoral Epistles describe it — what is the problem the gospel is answering?
elements.sampleAnswer: The gospel is necessary because of the human condition apart from God. Titus 3:3 gives a frank description of what we were: foolish, disobedient, misled, enslaved to various passions and desires, spending our lives in evil and envy, hateful and hating one another. Death reigns — which is why Christ's abolishing of death (2 Tim 1:10) is such good news. Human beings cannot save themselves — salvation is explicitly 'not because of any righteous deeds we did' (Titus 3:5) and 'not based on our works' (2 Tim 1:9). The gospel exists because God, in his love and grace, chose to intervene in a situation that human beings were powerless to remedy themselves.
elements.questionHint: Look at Titus 3:3 — Paul describes the human condition before the gospel arrives with striking honesty. What does that description tell you about why good news was needed? What was the problem the gospel came to solve?

elements.question: HOW has the gospel been implemented — in history and in our lives?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the historical events through which the gospel came (the incarnation, death, and resurrection of Christ) and the personal events through which it comes to individuals (new birth, the Spirit, faith)…
elements.sampleAnswer:
In history: the gospel was implemented through the appearing of Jesus Christ — his incarnation, his life, his death as a ransom for all (1 Tim 2:6), his resurrection which abolished death and brought life and immortality to light (2 Tim 1:10), and the pouring out of the Holy Spirit (Titus 3:6). These are real historical events, not myths or metaphors.
In our lives: the gospel comes through the washing of new birth and the renewing of the Holy Spirit (Titus 3:5) — a transformation that is entirely God's work. It is received through faith — 'I know the one in whom my faith is set' (2 Tim 1:12). And it is then lived out in a life of godliness, good works, and faithful witness (Titus 2:12; 3:8).
elements.questionHint: The 'how' of the gospel has two dimensions: the historical (what happened in Christ) and the personal (what happens in us). Try to address both. What events in history make the gospel possible, and what happens in a person's life when the gospel takes root?

elements.question: WHEN does the gospel have an impact? Is it a one-time event, or does it have an ongoing effect?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the past, present, and future dimensions of the gospel's impact — drawing on the passages you have studied…
elements.sampleAnswer:
The gospel has an impact across all of time:
In the past: it was planned before time began (2 Tim 1:9) and implemented in history through Christ's appearing, death, and resurrection. For individuals, it takes effect at the moment of new birth and justification.
In the present: the gospel continues to have an impact through the Spirit's ongoing work of renewal. It trains us to reject godlessness and live self-controlled, upright, and godly lives in the present age (Titus 2:12). It shapes how we treat others, how we use our gifts, and how we endure suffering.
In the future: the gospel gives us a confident hope — the glorious appearing of our great God and Saviour (Titus 2:13), and the inheritance of eternal life (Titus 3:7). This future hope shapes and sustains present faithfulness. The gospel is not a past event only; it is a living power with a past foundation, a present application, and a future completion.
elements.questionHint: Use the three-tense framework from question 5 above: past, present, future. The gospel is not only for the moment you first believe — where do you see its ongoing and future impact in the passages you have studied?

elements.question: WHERE did the gospel come from, and what places will it reach?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the ultimate origin of the gospel (eternity, God's own purpose) and its ultimate reach (all people, all nations)…
elements.sampleAnswer: The gospel comes from eternity — it was planned in God's own purpose before time began (2 Tim 1:9) and rooted in his eternal character: his love, kindness, and grace (Titus 3:4-5). It entered human history at a specific place and time through the incarnation of Jesus Christ. It was then entrusted to specific people — Paul as preacher and apostle (2 Tim 1:11; 1 Tim 2:7), and then to faithful people who would pass it on to others (2 Tim 2:2). Its reach is universal in scope: God wants all people to be saved and to come to a knowledge of the truth (1 Tim 2:4), and Christ gave himself as a ransom for all (1 Tim 2:6). The grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all people (Titus 2:11). The gospel is therefore both particular in its origin (one God, one mediator, one plan) and universal in its offer.
elements.questionHint: The 'where' question has two directions: where did the gospel come from (trace it back to its ultimate source), and where is it going (think about its intended scope and destination). Use the passages to answer both directions.

elements.text: 6.4 Step 3: Action to Take from a True Understanding of the Gospel

The gospel is God's good news of salvation from sin and evil through King Jesus — a declaration of what God has done. The message transforms those who have truly heard and understood it. Gordon Fee, in his commentary on the Pastoral Epistles, describes it as: "The good news of God's gracious acceptance and forgiveness of sinners, to which the proper response is faith (trusting God that he really does accept sinners) and love toward others." (Fee, 1 and 2 Timothy, Titus)

The next unit puts this reflection into practice through training us to share the gospel. But before moving on, consider what action you personally need to take.

elements.question: ATTITUDES: How does a true understanding of the gospel transform your view of yourself or of other people? Is there a change in your attitudes that this unit has revealed you need to make?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: SKILLS: Are there gaps in your ability to explain the gospel to others that you need to work on? What skills do you need to develop?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: KNOWLEDGE: Are there gaps in your understanding of the gospel that you need to fill? Do you have questions about the gospel that you need to have answered?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: End this unit in prayer. Use your new understanding of the gospel to pray for yourself — for a deeper grasp of the good news and for courage to live by it. Pray for your church — that it would be a community shaped by the gospel in its teaching, its relationships, and its mission. And pray for the community you are part of — that those who have not yet heard or understood the good news would come to know the one 'who abolished death and brought life and immortality to light through the gospel.'

This unit builds on Unit 6's reflection on the nature of the gospel and puts it into practice. We will examine why we should share the gospel and learn practical methods for doing so — including how to tell our own story, how to explain the gospel clearly, and how to live in a way that demonstrates its power.

elements.term: By the end of this unit you will be able to…
elements.question:
1. Name and then internalise clear motives for sharing the gospel
2. Share the story of what God has done in your life in a way that communicates the heart of the gospel
3. Use a simple framework to explain the gospel to others
elements.answer: Keep these outcomes in mind as you work through the unit. Return to them at the end to check your progress.

elements.question: What motivates you to share the good news of Jesus with other people?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: What hinders you in your desire and ability to share the gospel with others?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: 7.2 Why Should We Share the Gospel?
7.2.1 The Rule of God

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>I solemnly charge you before God and Christ Jesus, who will judge the living and the dead, and by his appearing and his kingdom: preach the message, be ready whether it is convenient or not, reprove, rebuke, exhort with complete patience and instruction.

elements.text:
The gospel is based on the fact that God is in charge of the world — he has purposes, makes plans, and is able to follow through on them. Paul calls Jesus 'our Lord' (2 Tim 1:8) and commands Timothy to preach 'in view of his appearing and his kingdom' (2 Tim 4:1). Jesus rules God's world and has the right to rule our lives.

The gospel is also for everyone, because God's rule extends over everyone. The church is designed to be a public address system — God's way of letting the whole world know the message and implications of the gospel. The rule of God means that the gospel is relevant and important for every person we meet. We have to share it — and share it well.

elements.question: How does the rule of God — the fact that Jesus is Lord over all — motivate us to share the gospel with all kinds of people, not just those who seem receptive?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what it means that Jesus has the right to rule every person's life — not just those who currently acknowledge him…
elements.sampleAnswer: If Jesus truly is Lord over all — if every person has been created under his rule and will one day stand before him as judge — then the gospel is not a private preference to be shared only with those who are interested. It is a public, urgent announcement that concerns every human being. The rule of God removes the option of treating the gospel as one lifestyle choice among many. It makes gospel-sharing a matter of love, responsibility, and faithfulness to the King who has commissioned his church to be his public address system to the world.
elements.questionHint: Think about what a 'public address system' does — it broadcasts a message whether or not the audience has asked to hear it, because the message concerns them. How does that image apply to the church's gospel responsibility?

elements.text: 7.2.2 The Love of God

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>But when the kindness of God our Savior and his love for humanity appeared, <sup>5</sup>he saved us not because of any righteous deeds we did but because of his mercy, through the washing of new birth and the renewing of the Holy Spirit.

elements.question: Think of some good news you have received in your own life. What did you do once you had received it? What does that instinct tell you about sharing the gospel?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think of a specific moment when you received genuinely good news — news about health, family, a job, a resolution. What was your first impulse?
elements.sampleAnswer: When we receive genuinely good news — a family member has recovered, a crisis has been resolved, something wonderful has happened — the natural instinct is to tell someone. Good news does not stay quietly contained; it overflows. The same logic applies to the gospel. If we have truly understood and received the good news of God's love in Christ, the most natural response is to want others to know it too. The gospel is not merely something we believe; it is something we have received — and received news gets shared. If we are not sharing it, it may be worth asking honestly whether we have truly grasped how good the news really is.
elements.questionHint: Try to be specific — recall a real moment of good news in your own experience. What did you actually do? Did you keep it to yourself, or did you find yourself wanting to tell people? Why?

elements.text: The Pastoral Epistles connect God's gospel directly with God's love. Paul writes of when 'the kindness and love of God our Saviour appeared' (Titus 3:4). The gospel is the good news of God's love, and good news is for sharing. The gospel speaks of God's love for us and transforms us to love others — and the most loving thing we can do for another person is to share the gospel of God's love with them.
7.2.3 The Justice of God

The gospel is a message of both God's love and God's justice. 2 Timothy 4:1 speaks of 'Christ Jesus, who will judge the living and the dead.' Our wrongdoing deserves punishment — but in love, Christ bears that shame and punishment himself. If people do not trust Christ to do that for them, they will one day face the consequences.

This is good news in one sense: God will not allow sin and injustice to go unpunished forever. But it is also a solemn warning for all people everywhere — and it gives us an urgent reason to share the gospel with those who have not yet heard.

elements.question: How do the three motivations — the rule, the love, and the justice of God — work together to create a compelling reason to share the gospel? Which of the three do you find most motivating personally, and why?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about how each motivation adds something the others lack — and then reflect honestly on which resonates most with you…
elements.sampleAnswer: The three motivations are complementary. The rule of God establishes the scope of the gospel's relevance — it concerns everyone, everywhere, because Jesus is Lord of all. The love of God establishes the spirit in which we share — not from duty or guilt but from overflow, as people who have received the most wonderful news imaginable. The justice of God establishes the urgency — the stakes are real and eternal, and those who never hear or understand the gospel face serious consequences. Together they guard against imbalance: rule alone can produce a hard, impersonal kind of evangelism; love alone can lose urgency; justice alone can produce fear without warmth. A gospel motivated by all three is both honest and attractive.
elements.questionHint: Consider what each motivation guards against: what kind of evangelism might result from having only one of these three motivations, without the other two? How do they balance and complete each other?

elements.text: 7.3 How Can We Share the Gospel?
7.3.1 A Testimony to Share

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>I am grateful to the one who has strengthened me, Christ Jesus our Lord, because he considered me faithful and put me into ministry, <sup>13</sup>even though I was formerly a blasphemer and a persecutor and an arrogant man. But I was treated with mercy because I acted ignorantly in unbelief, <sup>14</sup>and our Lord's grace was abundant, bringing faith and love in Christ Jesus. <sup>15</sup>This saying is trustworthy and deserves full acceptance: 'Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners' — and I am the worst of them! <sup>16</sup>But here is why I was treated with mercy: so that in me as the worst, Christ Jesus could demonstrate his utmost patience, as an example for those who are going to believe in him for eternal life. <sup>17</sup>Now to the eternal king, immortal, invisible, the only God, be honor and glory forever and ever! Amen.

elements.text:
A testimony is the presentation of evidence by a witness. When you give a testimony you are telling your story of what Christ has done in your life — it is, at its heart, a testimony about Christ. Paul's testimony in 1 Timothy 1:12-17 is a fine example: he moves from his own past (blasphemer, persecutor, arrogant) through the grace he received (treated with mercy, given faith and love in Christ) to the purpose his story serves (a demonstration of Christ's patience for others who will believe).

A well-prepared testimony follows a simple three-part structure:

elements.term: Before — During — After
elements.question: How do you structure a testimony that clearly communicates the gospel?
elements.answer:
1. Before — Explain what life was like before you met Christ. You may have been brought up in a Christian home where Jesus was always known. Do not invent a troubled past if you do not have one — but make it clear that you came to a point where you made a decision yourself about Christ.

2. During — How did you come to meet Christ and make that decision? What were the circumstances, who was influential, what got you thinking? All our stories are different — but make it clear that God calls us to turn away from our wrongdoing and self-centredness and to turn to him as our only hope.

3. After — What difference has it made? Be realistic about the ups and downs of the Christian life, but use examples of how Christ's love and God's promises have made a difference to you and those around you.

Practical tips: Prepare well — you will be more relaxed and yourself if you know your story. Keep the length appropriate for the listener. Avoid jargon: say 'rebellion and disobedience' rather than 'sin'; 'turn away from self and to God' rather than 'repent'; 'rely on what God has done in Christ rather than our own efforts' rather than 'faith'. Pray — changing lives is what God does, not us.

elements.question: Looking at Paul's testimony in 1 Timothy 1:12-17, identify the three parts of the Before–During–After structure. How does Paul's testimony keep Christ — rather than Paul himself — at the centre?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Identify what Paul says about his 'before', how he describes what happened, and what 'after' looks like in his testimony. Then notice: who is the hero of Paul's story?
elements.sampleAnswer:
Before: Paul describes his past with unflinching honesty — he was a blasphemer, a persecutor, and an arrogant man (v. 13). He does not soften or excuse it.
During: He was treated with mercy because he acted ignorantly in unbelief (v. 13). The Lord's grace was abundant, bringing faith and love in Christ Jesus (v. 14). The initiative is entirely God's.
After: Paul now serves in ministry, strengthened by Christ Jesus our Lord (v. 12). His life has become a demonstration of Christ's utmost patience — an example for all who will come to believe (v. 16).
Christ stays at the centre because Paul consistently attributes everything to God's action: 'the one who has strengthened me', 'I was treated with mercy', 'our Lord's grace was abundant'. Paul is present in the story, but as the recipient of grace, not as the hero. The story ends not with Paul but with a doxology to God (v. 17) — which is where every good testimony ultimately points.
elements.questionHint: Count how many active verbs in vv. 12-16 have Paul as the subject, and how many have God or Christ as the subject. What does that distribution tell you about who is really at the centre of a good testimony?

elements.text: The best way to prepare your testimony is to write it out and then practise sharing it with another Christian — ideally for around five minutes — asking for honest feedback on what was clear and what was not. The exercise below is designed to help you do that. If you are working through this unit alone, use the question to write a draft of your testimony in preparation for sharing it with someone soon.

elements.question: Write a draft of your own testimony using the Before–During–After structure. Aim for something you could share naturally in about five minutes. Avoid Christian jargon — write it as if explaining to someone who has never been to church.
elements.answerPlaceholder:
Before (what life was like before Christ):

During (how you came to faith):

After (what difference it has made):
elements.sampleAnswer: A good testimony draft will be personal and specific rather than generic. It will be honest about the 'before' without exaggerating. It will be clear about what actually happened — who or what God used — in the 'during', and will show genuine, concrete change in the 'after'. Most importantly, it will be about Christ more than about the speaker: the speaker's story is the frame, but Christ's grace is the picture. After drafting, read it aloud to check the length and to hear whether any jargon has crept in.
elements.questionHint: Try to be specific rather than vague — 'I was proud and self-sufficient' is clearer than 'I was a sinner'. 'A friend invited me to read the Gospel of John and I couldn't stop thinking about who Jesus claimed to be' is more gripping than 'I came to faith'. The more specific, the more believable and engaging.

elements.text: 7.3.2 A Message to Communicate

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>This saying is trustworthy and deserves full acceptance: 'Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners' — and I am the worst of them! <sup>16</sup>But here is why I was treated with mercy: so that in me as the worst, Christ Jesus could demonstrate his utmost patience, as an example for those who are going to believe in him for eternal life. <sup>17</sup>Now to the eternal king, immortal, invisible, the only God, be honor and glory forever and ever! Amen.

elements.text: We need to be able to communicate the gospel simply and explain it deeply. BUILD's 3-2-1 method is a simple framework for doing this, built on 1 Timothy 1:15-17. Take time to learn this passage by heart if you have not done so already. The framework is called 3-2-1 because the passage contains: 3 things to understand, 2 things to do, and 1 way to live.

elements.alt: The 3-2-1 gospel diagram showing three rows of simple hand-drawn pictures. Row 3 (top): three pictures showing a crown (God is King), a person separated from the crown by a line (we are sinners), and a person with a cross between them and the crown (Jesus saves). Row 2 (middle): two pictures showing an arrow pointing up from a person to a crowned cross (believe in Jesus) and an arrow pointing down from a crowned cross to a person (receive new life). Row 1 (bottom): one picture showing arrows pointing in all four directions from a person beneath a crowned cross (live God's way).
elements.caption: The 3-2-1 method illustrated: 3 things to understand, 2 things to do, 1 way to live

elements.term: 1. God is King   2. We are sinners   3. Jesus came to save us
elements.question: What are the three foundational truths of the gospel that every person needs to understand?
elements.answer:
1. God is King
God is 'the eternal king, immortal, invisible, the only God' (v. 17). God is in charge of everything and everyone — always has been and always will be, because he alone is eternal. He is not like us: he is immortal and invisible. He is unique — the only God. We have been created to give him honour and glory, which means recognising who he is and living to please him.
Question to consider: Do you understand that God is King and that you should obey him?

2. We are sinners
God made us to love and obey him, but we live for our own honour and glory. That is why Paul calls people 'sinners' and counts himself 'the worst of them' (vv. 15-16). No one likes this label — but Paul has just told us that God loves us very much (v. 14). Despite and because of his great love for us, God cannot stand our sin and we cannot approach him. This leads to death — a problem that only God can solve.
Question to consider: Do you understand that you are a sinner and are cut off from God?

3. Jesus came to save us
The heart of the 'trustworthy saying' is this: 'Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners' (v. 15). If we understand our guilt, shame and powerlessness, we know we need a saviour. The title 'Christ Jesus' means 'God's chosen rescuer.' God the King came in the person of his Son — and his death and resurrection completed his mission. At the cross, Jesus dealt with sin and evil once and for all.
Question to consider: Do you understand that Christ Jesus is the only one who can rescue you?

elements.term: 1. Believe in Jesus   2. Receive new life
elements.question: What are the two responses the gospel calls for?
elements.answer:
1. Believe in Jesus
Verse 16 reminds us that God has done all the work, but we need to respond: we must 'believe in him.' That means trusting what God has already done for us through Jesus Christ. The gospel is 'a trustworthy saying that deserves full acceptance' — we must put our hope fully in Jesus, the only one who can rescue us.
Question to consider: Will you put your trust fully in Jesus to save you?

2. Receive new life
Verse 16 continues: as we trust in God we 'receive eternal life.' The King removes our sin and shame and welcomes us into his kingdom — a place with no evil and no end. That life begins now through the gift of God's Holy Spirit and continues beyond death into the future, when God's new heavens and new earth will be established forever.
Question to consider: Will you open your heart to receive God's gift of new life?

elements.term: Live God's way
elements.question: What does the gospel call us to from now on?
elements.answer:
Believing in Jesus and receiving God's Holy Spirit is just the start. The passage ends: 'Now to the eternal king, immortal, invisible, the only God, be honour and glory forever and ever. Amen.' Paul learnt to love, honour and obey God — and we must do the same. That means loving others and living for God in every area of life.
Question to consider: Will you turn from sin and evil and live God's way from now on?

elements.question: In your own words, summarise the 3-2-1 framework as you would explain it to someone else. What makes this a useful structure for sharing the gospel, and what are its potential limitations?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Summarise the 3 things, 2 things, and 1 way in your own words. Then think about what the framework does well and where you might need to supplement it…
elements.sampleAnswer:
The 3-2-1 framework offers a clear, memorable, and biblically grounded way to explain the gospel. The three things to understand establish the necessary background: God's kingship, human sinfulness, and Christ's saving mission. The two things to do make clear that the gospel requires a response — belief and reception of new life — not merely intellectual agreement. The one way to live shows that the gospel transforms everything that follows, not just the moment of conversion.
Limitations: Any short gospel framework will simplify. This one focuses primarily on the individual's relationship with God; it could be supplemented with the gospel's corporate and cosmic dimensions (new community, new creation). The language of 'receiving eternal life' might also be misunderstood as purely future — it is worth emphasising that new life begins now. As the PDF notes, even Jesus and Paul explained the same gospel in different ways in different settings — the 3-2-1 method is a starting point, not a script.
elements.questionHint: Think about someone specific you might share the gospel with. How would you adapt the 3-2-1 framework for that person's background, questions, or objections? What would you need to add or explain differently?

elements.text: The best preparation for using the 3-2-1 method is to practise explaining it with another Christian before using it in an evangelistic context. The exercise below is designed to help with this. If you are working through this unit alone, use the question to write out a full gospel explanation in your own words — as if you were speaking to a specific person you know.

elements.question: Write out a gospel explanation using the 3-2-1 framework in your own words — as if you were speaking to a specific person you know who is not yet a Christian. Avoid jargon. Aim for something you could share naturally in about five minutes.
elements.answerPlaceholder:
3 things to understand:
1. God is King — 
2. We are sinners — 
3. Jesus came to save us — 

2 things to do:
1. Believe in Jesus — 
2. Receive new life — 

1 way to live:
Live God's way —
elements.sampleAnswer: A good gospel explanation using 3-2-1 will be clear, personal, and jargon-free. It will use plain language — 'rebellion' rather than 'sin', 'turning to God rather than depending on yourself' rather than 'repentance and faith'. It will keep returning to the biblical text (1 Tim 1:15-17) to show that the authority behind the message is God's, not the speaker's. And it will move naturally toward the response questions — not as a sales pitch but as genuine invitations to consider the claims of the gospel. After writing, read it aloud and listen for any technical words that crept in.
elements.questionHint: Think of someone real as your imagined audience. What do they believe about God? What questions or objections might they have? How would you explain 'God is King' to someone who has never thought about God at all, or to someone from a different religious background?

elements.term: A prayer to use with someone responding to the gospel
elements.question: The 3-2-1 framework naturally leads to an opportunity to help someone respond to the gospel. Here is a model prayer you can use or adapt.
elements.answer:
Heavenly Father, I recognise that you are King. I have been living in your world but I have not been living in your way. Thank you for sending Jesus to be my saviour. I trust in him alone for my salvation and receive the gift of life that your Holy Spirit brings. From now on I want to live for Jesus until I meet him face to face in his new world. Thank you for the gift of your Holy Spirit, who will strengthen me to live life your way from now on. In Jesus' name I pray. Amen.

You can adjust the content of the prayer for the situation of the listener or group. The key movements of the prayer follow the 3-2-1 structure: acknowledging God as King, confessing sin, trusting in Jesus, and committing to live God's way.

elements.text: 7.3.3 A Change to Demonstrate

The Pastoral Epistles make clear that the gospel is not only communicated in words — it is also demonstrated through lives that show the change Christ brings. There is a non-verbal dimension to evangelism that the spoken message cannot replace: the visible transformation of individuals and communities who have truly received the gospel.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>But as for you, communicate the behavior that goes with sound teaching. <sup>2</sup>Older men are to be temperate, dignified, self-controlled, sound in faith, in love, and in endurance. <sup>3</sup>Older women, likewise, are to exhibit behavior that reflects holiness, not to be slanderers, not to be excessive drinkers, but to teach what is good. <sup>4</sup>In this way they will train the younger women to love their husbands, to love their children, <sup>5</sup>to be self-controlled, pure, fulfilling their duties at home, kind, and submissive to their own husbands, so that the message of God will not be discredited. <sup>6</sup>Encourage younger men likewise to be self-controlled, <sup>7</sup>showing yourself to be an example of good works in every way. In your teaching show integrity, dignity, <sup>8</sup>and a sound message that cannot be criticized, so that any opponent will be ashamed, because he has nothing bad to say about us. <sup>9</sup>Slaves are to be submissive to their own masters in everything, to do what is wanted and not to talk back, <sup>10</sup>not to pilfer, but to demonstrate complete loyalty, so that in everything they will make the teaching about God our Savior attractive.

elements.question: What does Titus 2:1-10 say about the link between the message of the gospel and the lives of those who have received it? What kinds of behaviour does Paul connect directly to the credibility of the gospel?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at the different groups Paul addresses — older men, older women, younger women, younger men, slaves. What behaviour does he call for, and what is at stake if they don't live this way?
elements.sampleAnswer:
Titus 2:1-10 shows that Paul expects the gospel to produce visible, social transformation across every age group and social category. Older men are to model temperance, dignity and self-control. Older women are to live in holiness and to teach younger women. Younger women are called to love their families. Younger men — and Titus himself — are to model self-control and integrity. Even those in the most powerless social positions (slaves) are to demonstrate complete loyalty.
Two phrases reveal what is at stake: Paul says that without this transformed behaviour 'the message of God will be discredited' (v. 5), and with it believers 'will make the teaching about God our Saviour attractive' (v. 10). The lives of Christians are not incidental to evangelism — they are part of the message. A congregation whose internal life contradicts the gospel it preaches will find its words undercut by its behaviour.
elements.questionHint: Notice the repeated phrase 'so that' in the passage — it links behaviour to outcome. What outcomes does Paul say depend on how Christians live? What does that tell you about the relationship between conduct and credibility in evangelism?

elements.question: In what ways does your own life — and the life of your church or churches — demonstrate the impact of the gospel? Where does Titus 2:1-10 challenge you individually and corporately?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Be honest — where does your church's common life make the gospel attractive, and where might it be undermining the message you proclaim?
elements.sampleAnswer: Responses will be personal and contextual. The question is designed to provoke honest self-examination rather than produce a 'correct' answer. A useful prompt: think of the people in your community who observe your church from the outside — neighbours, family members of church members, local leaders. What impression of the gospel do they receive from what they see? Where is the community's life a commendation of the message, and where is there a gap between what is proclaimed and what is lived?
elements.questionHint: Think about specific areas: how your church handles money, conflict, the treatment of the poor, relationships between older and younger members, the integrity of its leaders. Which of these most clearly demonstrates the gospel — and which most urgently needs the gospel's transformation?

elements.text: 7.4 Conclusion

We have examined three motivations for sharing the gospel — the rule, the love, and the justice of God — and three methods: sharing our testimony, explaining the message, and demonstrating the change the gospel brings. None of these three methods stands alone: words without changed lives ring hollow; a changed life without words leaves people without the gospel; and a personal story without the gospel message is merely biography.

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>…so that in everything they will make the teaching about God our Savior attractive.

elements.text: End this unit in prayer — asking God to open the eyes of our hearts to see and understand his kingdom of love and justice, and to help us share his good news well. Pray specifically for the people in your life who have not yet heard or understood the gospel. Ask God to give you the words, the courage, the prepared story, and the changed life that will 'make the teaching about God our Saviour attractive' (Titus 2:10).
7.5 Extra: The Different Words for Sharing the Gospel

elements.question: What words or expressions do you use for the activity of sharing the gospel with others — and what words do others in your context use? List as many as you can think of, and reflect on what each one emphasises or implies.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think of all the words you have heard for this activity — 'evangelism', 'witnessing', 'preaching', 'testifying', 'sharing your faith', and others. What does each word imply about how the activity is done, and who does it?

elements.text: In this unit you will apply the four steps from Unit 4 to another passage from 2 Timothy — 2:1-7. Keep referring back to the four steps as you go. Think back to your earlier experience of this process — something you found particularly hard — and give that area extra attention this time.

elements.term: By the end of this unit you will be able to…
elements.question:
1. Follow and apply the four steps of Encountering a Bible Passage to a passage from 2 Timothy yourself
2. Share the message of 2 Timothy 2:1-7 with a group of people today
elements.answer: Keep these outcomes in mind as you work through the unit. Return to them at the end to check your progress.

elements.text: The Passage: 2 Timothy 2:1-7

Before you begin the four steps, read the passage carefully — more than once. Read it slowly and aloud if you can. This passage ends with the words 'reflect on what I am saying, for the Lord will give you insight into all this' — so take that invitation seriously before you begin. Notice in particular the three vivid images Paul uses: the soldier, the athlete, and the farmer. Sit with each one before you start to analyse.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>So you, my child, be strong in the grace that is in Christ Jesus. <sup>2</sup>And entrust what you heard me say in the presence of many others as witnesses to faithful people who will be competent to teach others as well. <sup>3</sup>Take your share of suffering as a good soldier of Christ Jesus. <sup>4</sup>No one in military service gets entangled in matters of everyday life; otherwise he will not please the one who recruited him. <sup>5</sup>Also, if anyone competes as an athlete, he will not be crowned as the winner unless he competes according to the rules. <sup>6</sup>The farmer who works hard ought to have the first share of the crops. <sup>7</sup>Think about what I am saying and the Lord will give you understanding of all this.

elements.text: Step 1: FLESH — Study the Details
1a) Set the Text in Context

elements.passageText:
13Hold to the standard of sound words that you heard from me and do so with the faith and love that are in Christ Jesus. 14Protect that good thing entrusted to you, through the Holy Spirit who lives within us.
15You know that everyone in the province of Asia has deserted me, including Phygelus and Hermogenes. 16May the Lord show mercy to the family of Onesiphorus, because he often refreshed me and was not ashamed of my imprisonment. 17But when he arrived in Rome, he eagerly searched for me and found me. 18May the Lord grant him to find mercy from the Lord on that day! And you know very well how many ways he served me in Ephesus.
2:1So you, my child, be strong in the grace that is in Christ Jesus. 2And entrust what you heard me say in the presence of many others as witnesses to faithful people who will be competent to teach others as well. 3Take your share of suffering as a good soldier of Christ Jesus. 4No one in military service gets entangled in matters of everyday life; otherwise he will not please the one who recruited him. 5Also, if anyone competes as an athlete, he will not be crowned as the winner unless he competes according to the rules. 6The farmer who works hard ought to have the first share of the crops. 7Think about what I am saying and the Lord will give you understanding of all this.
8Remember Jesus Christ, raised from the dead, a descendant of David; this is my gospel, 9for which I am suffering to the point of imprisonment as a criminal, but God's message is not imprisoned! 10So I endure all things for the sake of those chosen by God, that they too may obtain salvation in Christ Jesus and its eternal glory. 11This saying is trustworthy: If we died with him, we will also live with him. 12If we endure, we will also reign with him. If we deny him, he will also deny us. 13If we are unfaithful, he remains faithful, since he cannot deny himself.

elements.question: STORY: Where does 2 Tim 2:1-7 come in the argument of the book? What comes immediately before and after it, and how does knowing that help you understand these verses?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at what comes before (1:13-18) and after (2:8-13). What has Paul just said, and where is he heading next?
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage sits between two important sections. Before it (1:13-18), Paul has called Timothy to hold to sound words and guard the good deposit — and has given two contrasting examples: those in Asia who deserted Paul, and Onesiphorus who sought him out and was not ashamed of his imprisonment. The contrast sets up the appeal in 2:1-7: Timothy must be like Onesiphorus — strong, faithful, and unashamed. After our passage (2:8-13), Paul grounds everything in the gospel ('Remember Jesus Christ, raised from the dead') and the trustworthy saying about dying and living with Christ. The passage therefore sits at a hinge point: it calls Timothy to costly, faithful ministry (2:1-7) and then immediately grounds that call in the gospel that makes such endurance possible (2:8-13).
elements.questionHint: The word 'So' at the start of v. 1 connects back to something. What has Paul just described — the desertions and the faithfulness of Onesiphorus — and how does that set up the appeal that follows? And what does the gospel section in vv. 8-13 add to the call in vv. 1-7?

elements.question: SITUATION: What do you notice about 2 Tim 2:1-7 in relation to what you already know about the situation in 2 Timothy — the people, problems, and places involved?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about Paul's imprisonment, the desertions he has mentioned, Timothy's task, and the urgency of the moment. How does the situation shape what Paul says here?
elements.sampleAnswer: The situation shapes the passage directly. Paul is imprisoned and facing death; many have deserted him (1:15). In that context, the call to Timothy to 'be strong' (v. 1), to 'take your share of suffering' (v. 3), and to 'entrust' the gospel to faithful people who will teach others (v. 2) is urgent and personal. The three images — soldier, athlete, farmer — all carry the theme of costly, disciplined commitment to a long-term goal. They speak directly to a situation in which the easy path is to disengage, to avoid suffering, and to keep one's head down. Paul is asking Timothy to do the opposite: to press on, to multiply, and to endure.
elements.questionHint: Think about what Paul has just described: desertions, imprisonment, isolation. What would be the natural human response to that situation? And how does Paul's appeal to Timothy push against that natural response?

elements.question: STYLE: What features of 2 Tim 2:1-7 show that it is part of a personal letter? What do you notice about Paul's tone and the way he uses the three images?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the personal address, the use of images rather than abstract argument, and the closing invitation to reflect. What kind of writing is this — and how does the style serve the message?
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage is unmistakably a personal letter. Paul addresses Timothy as 'my child' (v. 1) — the same warm, fatherly language used throughout 2 Timothy. Instead of making abstract theological arguments, he speaks through vivid concrete images: the soldier, the athlete, the farmer. This is the language of a mentor who knows his apprentice well — who knows that Timothy will understand these images from everyday life and that they will stay with him longer than a proposition would. The closing invitation in v. 7 — 'Think about what I am saying, and the Lord will give you understanding' — is characteristic of the letter's intimate, personal tone: Paul does not do all the work for Timothy; he invites him to engage, reflect, and trust God for insight.
elements.questionHint: Why might Paul use three images rather than one? And why does he invite Timothy to 'think' and 'reflect' at the end, rather than spelling everything out? What does that tell you about Paul's approach to teaching and formation?

elements.text: 1b) See the Details of the Text

Read 2 Tim 2:1-7 again carefully. Look for: repeated and related words and phrases; important, complicated, or unusual words and phrases; and connections and links between words and phrases — including the 'So' that opens the passage, the three parallel images, and the closing invitation in v. 7.

elements.question: LIST the important words, phrases, and relationships you notice in 2 Tim 2:1-7. What stands out as you read carefully?
elements.answerPlaceholder: List words and phrases — one per line if that helps. Don't explain them yet, just notice and list them…
elements.sampleAnswer:
'So you, my child' (v. 1 — connecting word + personal address)
'Be strong in the grace that is in Christ Jesus' (v. 1 — the source of strength is Christ, not self)
'Entrust… to faithful people who will be competent to teach others' (v. 2 — the chain of gospel transmission)
'Many others as witnesses' (v. 2 — the public, witnessed nature of what has been passed on)
'Take your share of suffering' (v. 3 — costly involvement, not passive observation)
'Good soldier of Christ Jesus' (v. 3 — the first image)
'Not entangled in matters of everyday life' (v. 4 — focus, not distraction)
'Please the one who recruited him' (v. 4 — loyalty to the commanding officer)
'Competes according to the rules' (v. 5 — the second image; integrity, not just effort)
'Victor's crown' (v. 5 — reward for right competition)
'The farmer who works hard… first share of the crops' (v. 6 — the third image; labour before reward)
'Think about what I am saying' (v. 7 — invitation to reflect, not just receive)
'The Lord will give you understanding' (v. 7 — dependence on God for insight)
elements.questionHint: Try reading the passage three times: once for the three images, once for the linking and connecting words, once for repeated ideas. What do you see each time? Notice especially the relationship between v. 1 and v. 3 — what is Paul calling Timothy to, and on what basis?

elements.text: 1c) Seek the Meaning from the Details

elements.question: EXPLAIN the words, phrases, and relationships you listed. What do they mean in their context — both in today's language and in the original setting? What is their significance in the argument Paul is building?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Work through your list from the previous question. For each word or phrase: what does it mean, and why does it matter here?
elements.sampleAnswer:
'So you, my child' — the 'So' draws a conclusion from what has gone before: the desertions and the faithfulness of Onesiphorus. Timothy is being called to be like Onesiphorus, not like those who deserted. 'My child' maintains the warm, fatherly tone.
'Be strong in the grace that is in Christ Jesus' — the command is not 'find strength in yourself' but 'draw strength from what Christ has already given.' Grace is the source; strength is the result. This is crucial: the following demands are not to be met by sheer willpower.
'Entrust… to faithful people who will be competent to teach others' — this is the four-generation chain: Paul → Timothy → faithful people → others. The gospel must be passed on, not hoarded. 'Faithful' and 'competent to teach' are both required — character and ability together.
'Take your share of suffering' — not 'if suffering comes' but 'take your share' — suffering is expected and is to be embraced as part of faithful gospel ministry.
The soldier (vv. 3-4): a soldier's defining characteristic is single-minded loyalty. He does not let civilian distractions pull him away from his commanding officer's priorities. The image calls Timothy to undivided focus.
The athlete (v. 5): winning is not enough — winning correctly matters. The image calls Timothy to integrity, not just effort. Gospel ministry must be conducted according to God's rules, not our own shortcuts.
The farmer (v. 6): hard work comes before reward. The image calls Timothy to patient, unglamorous labour with trust that the harvest will come.
'Think about what I am saying… the Lord will give you understanding' — Paul does not spell out every application of the three images. He trusts Timothy to reflect prayerfully and trusts the Lord to give insight. This is itself a model of how to teach.
elements.questionHint: For the three images in particular, ask: what is the defining characteristic of each figure (soldier, athlete, farmer), and what aspect of gospel ministry does it illuminate? Try to state each one in a single sentence.

elements.text: 1d) Summarise Your Findings

elements.question: Drawing on your work so far, summarise your main findings from Step 1. What are the most important ideas you want to carry forward into the next steps?
elements.answerPlaceholder: What are the two or three most important things you have discovered about this passage so far?
elements.sampleAnswer:
Three main ideas emerge:
1. The call to strength grounded in grace — Timothy is called to demanding gospel ministry, but the source of his strength is not his own resolve; it is the grace that is in Christ Jesus. This is the foundation that makes everything else possible.
2. The call to multiply — the gospel must be entrusted to faithful, competent people who will teach others. Timothy is not simply to survive; he is to invest in the next generation of gospel workers. The chain must continue.
3. The call to costly, focused endurance — the three images (soldier, athlete, farmer) each illuminate a different dimension of what faithful gospel ministry requires: single-minded focus, integrity, and patient hard work before reward. Together they paint a picture of ministry that is costly, disciplined, and long-term in its horizon.
elements.questionHint: If you had to name the two or three most important things this passage is saying to Timothy — things you could carry into Steps 2-4 as a foundation — what would they be? Try to make each one a complete thought rather than a single word.

elements.text: Step 2: SKELETON — Outline the Structure

elements.question: LINKS: What important linking words contribute to the structure of 2 Tim 2:1-7? How do they show the logic of Paul's argument?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at 'so', 'and', 'also', 'similarly' — what does each link connect, and what does that tell you about the structure?
elements.sampleAnswer:
'So' (v. 1) — links back to the preceding section. The call to Timothy flows from what Paul has just described: the desertions, the faithfulness of Onesiphorus, and the call to guard the good deposit.
'And' (v. 2) — connects the call to personal strength (v. 1) to the call to multiplication (v. 2). Strength in grace is not self-contained; it overflows into investing in others.
'Take your share of suffering' (v. 3) — the word 'also' or 'join with me' in some translations links this to Paul's own suffering already described in chapter 1. Timothy is to share in what Paul is already experiencing.
'Also, if anyone competes as an athlete' (v. 5) — the 'also' signals a new image being added to the first. The images are parallel, not progressive: each illuminates a different facet of the same call.
'Think about what I am saying' (v. 7) — a summary connector: after presenting three images, Paul steps back and invites Timothy to draw his own conclusions with the Lord's help.
elements.questionHint: Linking words are the joints that hold the argument together. Try mapping the passage as a series of connected steps: what does Paul say first, what does he add, and how does he conclude? What would be lost if you removed the linking words?

elements.question: THEMES: How are the main ideas arranged in 2 Tim 2:1-7? Can you identify the major movements or sections in these verses?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Try to divide the passage into its main parts. What is Paul doing in vv. 1-2? In vv. 3-6? In v. 7?
elements.sampleAnswer:
The passage falls into three clear movements:
1. The foundation and the commission (vv. 1-2): Be strong in grace — and multiply. The basis for everything is the grace already given in Christ; the first application is to pass on what has been entrusted through faithful, competent people.
2. Three images of costly, disciplined ministry (vv. 3-6): The soldier (focus and loyalty), the athlete (integrity and right method), the farmer (hard work and patient waiting for reward). Each image illuminates a different quality required for faithful gospel ministry.
3. The invitation to reflect and depend (v. 7): Paul does not spell out every application. He trusts Timothy to think, and trusts the Lord to give understanding. This is itself a teaching about how formation works.
elements.questionHint: Try labelling each section with a single phrase that captures its function. Is Paul commanding? Illustrating? Inviting? What is he doing in each part — and how do the three parts relate to each other?

elements.question: OUTLINE: Having identified the 'big bones', write a simple structural outline of 2 Tim 2:1-7. What are the main sections and how would you label them?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Write your outline in 3 numbered points. Each point should capture one main section of the passage and explain what Paul is doing there…
elements.sampleAnswer:
1. The foundation and the first call (vv. 1-2): Be strong in the grace of Christ — and entrust the gospel to faithful people who will teach others. Gospel ministry begins with receiving and must overflow into multiplying.
2. Three images of what faithful gospel ministry looks like (vv. 3-6): The soldier (single-minded loyalty, no entanglement), the athlete (integrity, competing according to the rules), the farmer (hard work, patient waiting for the harvest). Each image adds a dimension to the portrait of a faithful gospel worker.
3. The call to reflect and depend on God for understanding (v. 7): Paul does not over-explain. He trusts Timothy's reflection and the Lord's illumination to complete the work of teaching.
elements.questionHint: A good outline is simple enough to remember and preach from. Aim for 3 points. Each should describe both what Paul is saying and what he is doing — the content and the function.

elements.text: Step 3: HEART — Identify the Message

elements.question: THEME: What is the main thing Paul is talking about in 2 Tim 2:1-7? State the theme of the passage as simply and clearly as you can — ideally in a short phrase.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Try to capture the theme in 3-6 words — the one subject this passage keeps coming back to…
elements.sampleAnswer: The theme is: faithful, costly, multiplying gospel ministry.
elements.questionHint: The theme is the subject of the passage — what it is about, before you say what it says about it. If you had to name the one thing this passage keeps returning to, what would it be?

elements.question: MESSAGE: What is Paul saying about that theme? Complete this sentence: 'Paul tells Timothy that…' Aim for one or two clear sentences, without technical language, that even a child could understand.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Complete the sentence: 'Paul tells Timothy that…'
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul tells Timothy that faithful gospel ministry — passing on what he has received, enduring suffering, and pressing on with focus, integrity, and patient hard work — is possible because the strength for it comes from the grace already given in Christ Jesus, not from Timothy himself.
elements.questionHint: The message should complete the theme: the theme says 'what about?'; the message says 'what about it?' Try to say it in plain language — something a ten-year-old could understand. No jargon, no technical terms.

elements.text: Step 4: BRAIN — Understand the Purpose

elements.question: PURPOSE: Why is Paul making this point to Timothy? What is he trying to achieve by saying this, in this situation, at this moment?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what Timothy needs and what danger Paul is guarding against. What would happen if Timothy did not hear this message?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul's purpose is to equip Timothy for the long haul of gospel ministry in a difficult situation — and to ensure that the gospel does not die with Paul or with Timothy, but multiplies through faithful people. Timothy is facing the twin temptations of discouragement (because of Paul's suffering and the desertions) and distraction (getting entangled in lesser things). Paul's three images address both: they call Timothy to press on with focus, integrity, and patience, grounded in grace. Without this message, Timothy might shrink back, disengage, or fail to invest in the next generation. With it, he has both a command and a framework for understanding what faithful ministry looks like.
elements.questionHint: The purpose answers 'why?' — what is the saying meant to achieve? What twin dangers is Paul guarding against in Timothy — discouragement and distraction? And what would be lost for the gospel if Timothy gave in to either?

elements.question: CONNECTIONS: What connections do you see between the purpose of 2 Tim 2:1-7 and your own situation and congregation today? Capture your answer as a statement or question that brings the passage into the present.
elements.answerPlaceholder: What do you and your people need to hear from this passage? Why do they need to hear it now?
elements.sampleAnswer: A connecting question might be: 'What would it look like for you to be a soldier, an athlete, and a farmer in your gospel ministry this week — and who are you investing in so that the gospel continues beyond you?' The three images are directly applicable to any leader in any context. The soldier speaks to the danger of distraction — the endless demands of everyday life that crowd out the priorities of gospel ministry. The athlete speaks to the danger of cutting corners — doing ministry in ways that are expedient rather than faithful. The farmer speaks to the danger of impatience — giving up before the harvest because the hard work doesn't seem to be producing results. And the multiplication principle of v. 2 speaks to every leader who has not yet identified and begun investing in the next generation of gospel workers.
elements.questionHint: Think about the specific pressures your congregation faces. Which of the three images speaks most directly to your situation — the soldier's distraction, the athlete's temptation to cut corners, or the farmer's struggle with patience? And who in your church or community could you be entrusting the gospel to?

elements.text: Responding to 2 Timothy 2:1-7

elements.question: How did the message of 2 Tim 2:1-7 speak to you personally? What did you learn, or what did you need to be reminded of?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: How did the purpose of the passage help you to apply God's word to your own life? What change might this passage make to your thinking or living?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: In your own words, state the message you would share with others from this passage. What is the main thing you want people to understand?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: Paul's closing words in this passage are an invitation as much as a command: 'Think about what I am saying, and the Lord will give you understanding of all this.' Take that seriously as you leave this unit. The insights from careful Bible study become fruitful through prayerful reflection. Ask the Lord to give you understanding — and trust that he will.

This unit flows out of our encounter with 2 Timothy 2:1-7 and explores how commitment to the gospel shapes the ministry of local church leaders. It introduces the BUILD 'ERA' model of theological reflection — a three-step process of Encounter, Reflection, and Action — which will be used throughout the BUILD curriculum.

elements.term: By the end of this unit you will be able to…
elements.question:
1. Recall and arrange the steps of theological reflection
2. Apply the steps of theological reflection to issues you encounter
3. Plan steps to make the gospel more central to your ministry
4. Use the acronym 'BUILD' to present the logic of gospel ministry
elements.answer: Keep these outcomes in mind as you work through the unit. Return to them at the end to check your progress.

elements.text: 9.1 Introducing Theological Reflection

We have seen that Timothy faced very real challenges — individuals distorting the gospel and damaging the church. We too face struggles and difficulties in our own ministries. Before we look at how to reflect well on those challenges, we need to name them honestly.

elements.question: Identify a challenge or problem you are currently facing in your ministry or church life. Describe it briefly — it might be a situation that is puzzling, a conflict, a question about direction, or a passage of Scripture that is challenging you.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Describe your challenge honestly. You will return to it as you work through the ERA steps later in this unit…

elements.question: Why do you think so much teaching and learning fails to bring about real change in people's lives? Where do you think the problem lies?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about teaching you have received or given — what made it stick, and what made it slide away without leaving a mark?
elements.sampleAnswer: Teaching and learning fail to produce change for several common reasons. First, there is often a gap between understanding something intellectually and genuinely wrestling with how it applies to a specific situation. Knowledge that remains abstract rarely changes behaviour. Second, there is rarely enough time or space for reflection — teaching moves quickly from one point to the next without pausing for the learner to process and internalise. Third, there is no plan for action: the session ends without identifying specific, concrete next steps. Fourth, the heart is not engaged — head knowledge without heart engagement produces information but not transformation. And fifth, prayer is often absent — the recognition that real change is God's work, not ours, and that we need his help to apply what we have heard.
elements.questionHint: Think about the last time you heard a genuinely good sermon or teaching session. How much of it are you still applying six months later? What happened in the gap between hearing and doing?

elements.question: How can we make sure that our encounters with the situations we face — and the Scriptures God has given us — lead to real learning and action rather than quickly forgotten insights?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about habits, structures, and practices that could bridge the gap between encounter and change…
elements.sampleAnswer: Several things help bridge the gap. Taking time to reflect before acting — sitting with what we have encountered rather than rushing to conclusions. Writing things down — the act of articulating observations and discoveries makes them more concrete and memorable. Working in community — others can challenge our blind spots and hold us accountable. Setting specific, measurable action steps rather than vague intentions. And above all, prayer — because genuine change is not something we can produce by effort alone; it requires the Holy Spirit's work in us. The ERA model introduced in this unit is designed to build all of these practices into a structured, repeatable process.
elements.questionHint: Think about the difference between passive and active learning. What does active learning require that passive listening does not? And what role does community play in making learning stick?

elements.passageText: <sup>7</sup>Think about what I am saying and the Lord will give you understanding of all this.

elements.text: Paul knew that reflection was essential for real learning to take place and for action to happen. And so he ends 2 Tim 2:1-7 with a command that comes with a wonderful promise: 'Think about what I am saying, and the Lord will give you understanding of all this' (2 Tim 2:7). Take time to memorise this verse — and consider the importance of careful reflection and the Lord's kindness in opening the eyes of our hearts to his truths.
9.2 The Three Steps of Theological Reflection

Theological reflection is a three-step process that encourages genuine learning and action:
  1. It begins when we encounter something in God's world and/or God's word with a desire for wisdom.
  2. It continues with reflection on those situations and Scriptures in order to gain wisdom.
  3. It leads to action in God's world with the wisdom that comes from God's word.
We will explore each step in turn — and apply them to the challenge you identified at the start of this unit.
9.2.1 Step 1: Encounter

The first step is to encounter something in God's world and God's word with a desire for wisdom.

Theological reflection is an encounter with Scripture or experience. Scripture always takes priority when it comes to shaping belief and behaviour — but we always encounter God's word in the context of the world around us. Timothy was called to reflect on God's word in the midst of the situation he faced. We too are to reflect on God's word in the context of all that is going on in our lives, churches, and communities.

In the discipline of encounter, you first describe things and then discover things. The goal of this step is not yet to explain or act — it is to see clearly and list what you find.

elements.question:
ENCOUNTER — DESCRIBE: Apply the following questions to the challenge or issue you identified at the start of this unit.

1. How did events unfold? Tell the story of what you experienced.
2. Who was involved? Describe the people.
3. What were the various people doing and saying? Record what happened.
4. Where and when did the events take place? Describe the situation.
5. What key things have you discovered that will need further reflection?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Work through each question in turn. Describe — don't yet explain or analyse. Resist the urge to jump to conclusions or solutions at this stage…
elements.sampleAnswer: The quality of this step depends entirely on honest, careful description rather than hasty interpretation. A common mistake is to skip past description into explanation too quickly — to say 'the problem is X' before fully describing what actually happened. Good encounter work notices details: the specific words people used, the sequence of events, the people present, the context and timing. It lists discoveries without yet ranking or explaining them. The test of a good encounter description is whether someone who was not present could understand exactly what happened from your account — and whether you yourself have noticed things you might otherwise have rushed past.
elements.questionHint: Resist the urge to explain or fix at this stage. Your job in this step is simply to see clearly and describe accurately. Imagine you are a reporter writing up what happened — facts first, interpretation later.

elements.text: 9.2.2 Step 2: Reflection

The second step is to reflect on those situations and the Scriptures in order to gain wisdom.

In 2 Tim 2:7, Paul commands Timothy to 'think about' (or 'reflect on') what he has said about gospel ministry. Any insight that results is a gift from 'the Lord' — which means we need to make space for God's grace through prayer in this process. Do not rely on your own understanding alone.

The most important thing is to keep the gospel at the centre of your reflection: to consider the situation in the light of God's good news. In the discipline of reflection, you consider your discoveries and develop principles.

elements.question:
REFLECTION — CONSIDER AND DEVELOP PRINCIPLES: Apply the following questions to the discoveries you made in Step 1.

1. Why might these things be happening? What reasons might lie behind what you described?
2. What light does the gospel — the good news of God's grace — shed on all this?
3. What Bible passages and themes are particularly relevant to the problem?
4. What pictures or illustrations help you to understand God's perspective on it?
5. What principles emerge from this process?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Work through each question in turn. Keep the gospel at the centre. List principles rather than plans — you will make plans in Step 3…
elements.sampleAnswer: Good reflection moves from description (Step 1) to explanation and principle (Step 2). It asks 'why?' rather than just 'what?' It looks for the deeper currents beneath the surface events. Most importantly, it brings the gospel to bear: not as a proof-text to close down thinking, but as a light that illuminates why things are the way they are and what God's perspective might be. A principle is a statement of truth that has wider application than the specific situation — it is something that could be carried into other contexts. For example: 'When leaders fail to model what they preach, their words lose credibility' or 'Fear of conflict often prevents churches from addressing problems early, allowing them to become crises.' Good principles are grounded in Scripture, honest about reality, and generative — they open up ways of thinking and acting rather than closing them down.
elements.questionHint: The key discipline of this step is keeping the gospel at the centre. For each discovery from Step 1, ask: how does the good news of what God has done in Christ shed light on this? What does it mean that God is sovereign, gracious, and faithful — in the context of this specific situation?

elements.text: 9.2.3 Step 3: Action

The final step is action in God's world with the wisdom that comes from God's word.

Paul calls Timothy to reflect on his words — which are God's words — for a purpose: so that he can be strengthened for a demanding ministry. Action is therefore not just about what you do but why you do it — it is to be guided by the gospel. It is tempting to leave principles as interesting topics for discussion, as many academics do. But rather than leaving them as theories on a page, you need to put what you have learnt into practice.

In the discipline of action, you plan new approaches to ministry, test things out, and develop new practice.

elements.question:
ACTION — PLAN AND PRACTISE: Apply the following questions to the principles you drew out in Step 2.

1. How might all this change my attitudes?
2. What action can I take that will put these principles into practice?
3. What plans can I make in order to achieve this?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Be specific and concrete. What will you actually do — and when? Vague intentions rarely lead to change; specific plans do…
elements.sampleAnswer: Good action planning is specific, realistic, and accountable. The test of a good action plan is whether it could be put in a diary — whether it names a concrete thing to do, by a specific time, in a measurable way. Vague actions ('I will pray more about this', 'I will try to be more encouraging') rarely produce change. Specific actions do: 'I will meet with X on Thursday and apologise for Y', 'I will begin a Bible study on this theme with my leadership team in the next two weeks', 'I will identify one person to invest in and meet with them monthly for six months.' Notice also that the first question asks about attitudes, not just activities — real change often begins with a shift in how we see a situation before it shows up in what we do.
elements.questionHint: Test your action plan: is it specific enough to put in a diary? Does it name a person, a time, and a concrete activity? And is it grounded in the gospel principles you identified in Step 2 — or is it just a practical fix that could have been thought of without any reflection at all?

elements.term: The three steps of theological reflection at a glance
elements.question: What is the definition, activity, and outcome of each of the three ERA steps?
elements.answer:
Step 1: ENCOUNTER something in God's world and God's word with a desire for wisdom.
Definition: To meet something new and surprising.
Activity: Describe.
Outcome: Discoveries.

Step 2: REFLECTION on those situations and the Scriptures in order to gain wisdom.
Definition: Pause for thought and go deeper.
Activity: Consider.
Outcome: Principles.

Step 3: ACTION in God's world with the wisdom that comes from God's word.
Definition: Do something new or different.
Activity: Plan.
Outcome: Practice.

elements.text: 9.3 Principles for Keeping the Gospel Central

We have looked at issues we are facing in the light of the gospel, and we have been challenged to keep the gospel central in our life and ministry.
