One way of listing, considering, and remembering some of the principles we have discovered is to use the letters that make up the word GOSPEL — G O S P E L — as a framework.

elements.question:
Using the letters G-O-S-P-E-L, identify principles for keeping the gospel central to your life and ministry. For each letter, find a word or short phrase that captures an important principle — and explain briefly what it means in practice.

G —
O —
S —
P —
E —
L —
elements.answerPlaceholder: Work through each letter. You might find more than one word for some letters — choose the one that most resonates with the principles you have discovered in this unit…
elements.sampleAnswer:
There is no single correct answer — the exercise is designed to help you find words that resonate with your own discoveries. Here are some starting suggestions:

G — God: keeping the gospel central means honouring God. His will and his ways matter more than anything else. Or: Grace — everything flows from what God has freely given, not from what we achieve.

O — Others: the gospel is not for us alone. Keeping it central means looking outward — to those who have not yet heard, and to those we are called to invest in.

S — Scripture: the gospel comes to us through God's word. Keeping it central requires constant return to Scripture as our authority and our nourishment.

P — Prayer: the ERA model places prayer at the heart of the process. Keeping the gospel central requires dependence on God, not reliance on our own understanding. Or: Perseverance — faithful gospel ministry is a long-term commitment.

E — Endurance: Paul's three images in 2 Tim 2:1-7 all point to costly, patient faithfulness. The gospel sustains us for the long haul. Or: Entrust — the gospel must be passed on to faithful people who will teach others.

L — Love: the gospel transforms us to love God and love others. Or: Leadership — those who have received the gospel must develop others to carry it forward.
elements.questionHint: Let your words emerge from the ERA process you have just worked through — from the principles you identified in Step 2 and the actions you planned in Step 3. The best words will be ones that feel personally significant, not just theologically correct.

elements.text: 9.4 ERA: A Further Look at the Steps of Theological Reflection

In English, the word ERA is a simple and memorable way of recalling the three steps: E is for Encounter, R is for Reflection, A is for Action.

The word 'era' means a significant period of history — the reign of a king is known as the era of that ruler. This is why ERA is such a fitting name for this cycle: the reason we wrestle with the situations we face in the light of the gospel, and then return to the often difficult context of God's world to take action in a renewed way, is to bring about change and transformation. The change we are seeking is that God's 'will be done, on earth as it is in heaven' (Matt 6:10) — to point to God's kingdom, the new era God is bringing in and through his king Jesus.

Having encountered God's world and God's word, we reflect and then act. But as we take action in God's world with the wisdom of God's word, we will make fresh discoveries and have new experiences. Those new encounters will deepen our reflection and refine our action. This means that the three steps of Encounter, Reflection, and Action form a continuing cycle — not a one-time process. Prayer lies at the heart of the cycle throughout, because the desire for wisdom must be continuous: 'If any of you lacks wisdom, you should ask God, who gives generously to all without finding fault, and it will be given to you.' (James 1:5)

elements.alt: The ERA cycle diagram: three boxes arranged in a triangle. At the top left is '1. Encounter'. At the top right is '2. Reflection'. At the bottom centre is '3. Action'. Arrows connect them in a cycle: Encounter leads to Reflection, Reflection leads to Action, Action leads back to Encounter. The word 'Prayer' sits at the centre of the triangle.
elements.caption: The ERA cycle: Encounter — Reflection — Action, with Prayer at the centre

elements.question: Why is it important to understand ERA as a cycle rather than a one-time process? What happens to our ministry if we only go through the three steps once and then stop?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what changes when we take action — and how that action creates new situations that need to be encountered, reflected on, and acted on again…
elements.sampleAnswer: Understanding ERA as a cycle is important because ministry is not a problem to be solved once — it is an ongoing relationship with God, his word, and his people in a changing world. When we take action, that action itself becomes a new experience to encounter: it produces results (expected and unexpected), raises new questions, and opens up new situations that require further reflection. A one-time process would treat ministry as static; the cycle reflects the dynamic reality that faithful gospel ministry requires continuous learning, continuous prayerful reflection, and continuous willingness to adjust and grow. Leaders who treat reflection as a one-time exercise tend to become rigid and stale; those who practise it as a cycle tend to remain responsive, humble, and growing.
elements.questionHint: Think about a decision you made in ministry that seemed right at the time but needed to be revisited later. What happened when you took action — and what did the results of that action teach you? How did that new learning feed back into further reflection?

elements.text: 9.5 Extra: The BUILD Logic of Gospel Ministry

In Unit 1 we learnt the meaning of the BUILD acronym. Here we build on that by seeing that the order in which the letters B-U-I-L-D come teaches an important logic of gospel ministry — a pattern we have been discovering throughout this unit.

elements.term: The logic and order of gospel ministry
elements.question: What is the logic behind the order of the BUILD letters — and how do they form a cycle of gospel ministry?
elements.answer:
1. Biblical — The Bible and its gospel are of immense value: 'All Scripture…' (2 Tim 3:16-17)
First, the Bible is a great treasure that bears God's authority. We must constantly return to Scripture.

2. Understanding — We must use it all with great care: 'Correctly handles…' (2 Tim 2:15)
Second, we must handle the glorious gospel it contains — the word of truth — with great care and integrity.

3. In-service — That includes using it in practice in mission: 'Reflect…' (2 Tim 2:7)
Third, careful use of Scripture is not simply about preserving the gospel — it is about sharing it and being strengthened by it. The Bible is God's gift for a church on mission.

4. Leadership — And passing true gospel ministry on to others: 'Entrust…' (2 Tim 2:2)
Fourth, part of that mission is to pass the gospel on — to train trustworthy and true ministers of the gospel.

5. Development — This is an ongoing commitment: 'Continue…' (2 Tim 3:14)
Fifth and finally, all this is an ongoing process. All true teachers of the gospel must continue in what they have learnt, watching both life and doctrine. And then the cycle returns to Biblical — back to Scripture, to renew our grasp of the gospel, and to begin again.

elements.alt: The BUILD learning cycle diagram: five boxes arranged in a circle with arrows connecting them clockwise. Biblical (All Scripture…) leads to Understanding (Correctly handles…) leads to In-service (Reflect…) leads to Leadership (Entrust…) leads to Development (Continue…) which leads back to Biblical.
elements.caption: The BUILD learning cycle: Biblical → Understanding → In-service → Leadership → Development → (and back to Biblical)

elements.question: In your own words, explain the logic of the BUILD cycle. Why does it matter that Biblical comes first? And why does Development loop back to Biblical rather than ending?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Try to explain the logic to someone who has not seen it before — why is the order important, and what does the cycle shape of the diagram tell us?
elements.sampleAnswer: Biblical comes first because everything else in the cycle depends on it. If our understanding of the gospel is distorted, our in-service practice will be distorted, our leadership will be distorted, and our development will reinforce the distortion rather than correct it. The Bible and its gospel are the standard against which everything else is measured — not one resource among many but the foundation. Development loops back to Biblical rather than ending because faithful gospel ministry is never complete in this life. Every stage of growth sends us back to Scripture with new questions, new situations, and new needs — and Scripture, rightly understood, sends us back out into mission and leadership again. The cycle reflects the rhythm of a lifetime of gospel ministry: return to Scripture, renew your understanding, go out in mission, invest in others, continue — and then return to Scripture again.
elements.questionHint: Think about what would happen if you started the cycle at a different point — at Leadership, for example, or at In-service. What would be missing? And what does it tell you that the cycle returns to Biblical rather than ending at Development?

elements.question: Which step of the BUILD cycle do you find most challenging in your own ministry — Biblical, Understanding, In-service, Leadership, or Development? Why? Pray about what that challenge reveals.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: Theological reflection is not a technique — it is a posture. It is the posture of a person who takes both God's word and God's world seriously, who refuses to react without thinking, and who trusts that the Lord will give understanding to those who seek it (2 Tim 2:7). May the ERA cycle — Encounter, Reflection, Action — become a natural rhythm of your ministry, and may the BUILD cycle keep drawing you back to Scripture as the wellspring of everything you do.

This unit applies the four steps from Unit 4 to 2 Timothy 3:14-17 — one of the most significant passages in the whole module. You have already met this passage as a reference point in Units 1 and 9, where 2 Tim 3:16-17 anchors the B (Biblical) in the BUILD acrostic. Now you have the chance to study it properly for the first time: to encounter it carefully, trace its structure, identify its message, and understand its purpose.

elements.term: Refer back to Unit 4 for the full learning outline.
elements.question: By the end of this unit you will be able to…
elements.answer:
1. Follow and apply the four steps of Encountering a Bible Passage to 2 Timothy 3:14-17
2. Share the message of 2 Timothy 3:14-17 with a group of people today

elements.text: The Passage: 2 Timothy 3:14-17

Before you begin the four steps, read the passage carefully — more than once. Read it slowly and aloud if you can. Pay attention to the contrast Paul sets up with the words 'But as for you…' — and notice what that contrast is built on. Then begin Step 1.

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>You, however, must continue in the things you have learned and are confident about. You know who taught you <sup>15</sup>and how from childhood you have known the holy scriptures, which are able to give you wisdom for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus. <sup>16</sup>Every scripture is inspired by God and useful for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, <sup>17</sup>that the person dedicated to God may be capable and equipped for every good work.

elements.text: Step 1: FLESH — Study the Details
1a) Set the Text in Context
Note: Verses 10-13 provide important background to 3:14-17 and the contrast made there ('But as for you…'). Read the wider passage carefully before answering the context questions below.

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>But you have followed my teaching, my way of life, my purpose, my faith, my patience, my love, my endurance, <sup>11</sup>as well as the persecutions and sufferings that happened to me in Antioch, in Iconium, and in Lystra. I endured these persecutions and the Lord rescued me from them all. <sup>12</sup>Now in fact all who want to live godly lives in Christ Jesus will be persecuted. <sup>13</sup>But evil people and charlatans will go from bad to worse, deceiving others and being deceived themselves. <sup>14</sup>You, however, must continue in the things you have learned and are confident about. You know who taught you <sup>15</sup>and how from childhood you have known the holy scriptures, which are able to give you wisdom for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus. <sup>16</sup>Every scripture is inspired by God and useful for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, <sup>17</sup>that the person dedicated to God may be capable and equipped for every good work.

elements.question: STORY: Where does 2 Tim 3:14-17 come in the argument of the book? What comes immediately before it (vv. 10-13), and how does that context shape the meaning of the contrast 'But as for you…' in v. 14?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look carefully at vv. 10-13. Who is Paul contrasting Timothy with, and what has been happening? What does 'But as for you…' push against?
elements.sampleAnswer: Verses 10-13 set up a sharp contrast. Paul describes two groups in close proximity. On one side: Paul himself — his teaching, way of life, purpose, faith, patience, love, and endurance, including the persecutions he suffered in Antioch, Iconium, and Lystra (vv. 10-11). Then Paul makes the sobering observation that all who want to live godly lives in Christ Jesus will be persecuted (v. 12). On the other side: 'evil people and charlatans' who go from bad to worse, deceiving others and being deceived themselves (v. 13). The contrast 'But as for you…' in v. 14 is therefore a direct push against the trajectory of the charlatans: Timothy must not go from bad to worse but must continue. The word 'continue' is the key — not backwards, not sideways, but forward in what he has already learned and been convinced of. The context makes clear that continuing is costly and counter-cultural: it means enduring persecution rather than drifting toward deception.
elements.questionHint: The phrase 'But as for you' appears twice in this chapter — look at both occurrences (v. 10 and v. 14). What is Paul contrasting Timothy with each time? What two trajectories is he mapping out, and which one is Timothy called to follow?

elements.question: SITUATION: What do you notice about 2 Tim 3:14-17 in relation to what you already know about the situation in 2 Timothy — the people, problems, and places involved?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the false teachers, the desertions, Paul's imprisonment, and Timothy's need to stand firm. How does the situation shape what Paul says about Scripture here?
elements.sampleAnswer: The situation in 2 Timothy is one of crisis: false teachers are spreading, people are deserting Paul, and Timothy must carry on without the apostle's physical presence. In that context, Paul's appeal to Scripture is not abstract theology — it is a practical lifeline. The false teachers and charlatans of vv. 1-13 are precisely the kind of people who distort or ignore Scripture. Over against them, Paul points Timothy to the Scriptures he has known since childhood (v. 15) as the reliable, God-breathed resource that will equip him for every good work (v. 17). The situation makes the passage urgent: Timothy needs something he can anchor to when Paul is gone and the winds of false teaching are blowing. Paul's answer is: anchor to Scripture.
elements.questionHint: Think about what Timothy is facing without Paul — false teachers, deserting colleagues, a hostile world. What does he need most in that situation? And how does Paul's description of Scripture in vv. 15-17 speak directly to those specific needs?

elements.question: STYLE: What features of 2 Tim 3:14-17 show that it is part of a personal letter? What do you notice about Paul's tone — and the way he moves from the personal ('you know who taught you') to the universal ('every scripture is inspired by God')?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the personal address, the appeal to Timothy's own history, and the shift to a general statement about all Scripture. What kind of argument is Paul making?
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage is deeply personal in its opening: 'You, however, must continue…' and 'You know who taught you' (vv. 14-15). Paul is not making an abstract point about Scripture — he is reminding Timothy of his own story, his own formation, the specific people who shaped him (his grandmother Lois and mother Eunice, as mentioned in 1:5). The appeal to Timothy's personal history gives the passage its warmth and its persuasive force. Then in v. 16, Paul shifts from the personal to the universal: 'Every scripture is inspired by God…' — a statement that applies not just to Timothy's childhood formation but to all of God's written word, for all believers. This movement from particular to general is characteristic of Paul's teaching method in letters: he grounds universal truth in personal and historical particulars, making it concrete before making it comprehensive.
elements.questionHint: Notice the two-stage movement in the passage: vv. 14-15 are addressed to Timothy's specific personal history, while vv. 16-17 make a universal claim about all Scripture. Why might Paul have arranged it this way? What does the personal grounding add to the universal statement?

elements.text: 1b) See the Details of the Text

Read 2 Tim 3:14-17 again carefully. Look for: repeated and related words and phrases; important, complicated, or unusual words and phrases; and connections and links — including the opening contrast ('You, however…'), the purpose clause in v. 17 ('so that…'), and the four uses of Scripture listed in v. 16.

elements.question: LIST the important words, phrases, and relationships you notice in 2 Tim 3:14-17. What stands out as you read carefully?
elements.answerPlaceholder: List words and phrases — one per line if that helps. Don't explain them yet, just notice and list them…
elements.sampleAnswer:
'You, however' (v. 14 — contrast, sets Timothy apart from the charlatans of vv. 10-13)
'Must continue' (v. 14 — a command; not optional, not passive)
'Learned and are confident about' (v. 14 — both knowledge and conviction)
'You know who taught you' (v. 14 — appeal to personal history and trusted relationships)
'From childhood you have known the holy scriptures' (v. 15 — a long, deep formation)
'Able to give you wisdom for salvation' (v. 15 — Scripture's primary saving purpose)
'Through faith in Christ Jesus' (v. 15 — salvation is through Christ, not Scripture itself)
'Every scripture' (v. 16 — comprehensive scope)
'Inspired by God' (v. 16 — literally 'God-breathed'; the divine origin of Scripture)
'Useful for…' (v. 16 — the practical, functional value of Scripture)
'Teaching… reproof… correction… training in righteousness' (v. 16 — four uses; a complete picture)
'So that' (v. 17 — purpose clause; links the usefulness of Scripture to its goal)
'The person dedicated to God' (v. 17 — the target recipient)
'Capable and equipped for every good work' (v. 17 — the outcome; complete preparation)
elements.questionHint: Try reading the passage three times: first for key words and phrases, then for linking and contrasting words, then for the structure of the argument. What do you notice each time? Pay particular attention to v. 16 — how many distinct things does Paul say about Scripture in that single verse?

elements.text: 1c) Seek the Meaning from the Details

elements.question: EXPLAIN the words, phrases, and relationships you listed. What do they mean in their context — both in today's language and in the original setting? What is their significance in the argument Paul is building?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Work through your list from the previous question. For each word or phrase: what does it mean, and why does it matter here?
elements.sampleAnswer:
'You, however' — signals a sharp contrast. Over against the deceivers who go from bad to worse, Timothy must go in the opposite direction: forward in what he has been taught.
'Must continue' — this is not a gentle suggestion; it is a command. The verb carries the sense of remaining in, persisting in, not abandoning. The danger of drift is real.
'Learned and are confident about' — two dimensions are required: intellectual knowledge and personal conviction. Head and heart together. One without the other is insufficient.
'You know who taught you' — the appeal is to trusted, known relationships — specifically the chain from Paul through Lois and Eunice to Timothy. The reliability of the teaching is grounded in the reliability of the teachers.
'From childhood you have known the holy scriptures' — Timothy's formation was long and deep. This is not a recent acquaintance but a lifelong relationship with God's word. 'Holy scriptures' here refers primarily to the Old Testament.
'Able to give you wisdom for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus' — the primary purpose of Scripture is salvific: it leads to wisdom for salvation. But crucially, that salvation comes 'through faith in Christ Jesus' — Scripture points to Christ; it does not replace him.
'Every scripture is inspired by God' — the Greek is theopneustos: 'God-breathed'. Scripture originates in God's own breath — his Spirit. This is the strongest possible claim for Scripture's authority and reliability.
'Useful for teaching, reproof, correction, training in righteousness' — four complementary functions. Teaching establishes right belief; reproof exposes wrong belief; correction restores right direction; training in righteousness forms right character and practice. Together they cover the full scope of spiritual formation.
'So that the person dedicated to God may be capable and equipped for every good work' — the purpose clause reveals the goal of all Scripture's usefulness: not knowledge for its own sake, but complete preparation for ministry. 'Every good work' is comprehensive — nothing is outside Scripture's equipping reach.
elements.questionHint: For the four uses of Scripture in v. 16, try to explain each one in a single plain sentence, and give an example of what it would look like in practice. Then ask: is any one of the four more important than the others, or do they work together as a complete set?

elements.text: 1d) Summarise Your Findings

elements.question: Drawing on your work so far, summarise your main findings from Step 1. What are the most important ideas you want to carry forward into the next steps?
elements.answerPlaceholder: What are the two or three most important things you have discovered about this passage so far?
elements.sampleAnswer:
Three main ideas emerge:
1. The command to continue — Timothy is called to persist in what he has learned and been convinced of, over against the drift and deception of the charlatans. Continuing is costly and counter-cultural; it requires both knowledge and conviction, both personal formation and trusted community.
2. The nature of Scripture — 2 Tim 3:16-17 makes the strongest possible claim for Scripture: it is God-breathed, comprehensive in scope ('every scripture'), and practically useful across four dimensions of spiritual formation. It is not merely a human document about God; it is God's own word, bearing his authority.
3. The purpose of Scripture — Scripture's usefulness is not an end in itself; it serves a clear goal: the complete equipping of the person dedicated to God for every good work. Scripture is not for academic study alone but for the formation and deployment of faithful gospel workers.
elements.questionHint: If you had to name the two or three most important things this passage is saying — things you could carry into Steps 2-4 as a foundation — what would they be? Try to make each one a complete thought, not just a single word.

elements.text: Step 2: SKELETON — Outline the Structure

elements.question: LINKS: What important linking words contribute to the structure of 2 Tim 3:14-17? How do they show the logic of Paul's argument?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at 'you, however', 'and', 'which', 'every', 'and', 'so that' — what does each link connect, and what does that tell you about how the argument is built?
elements.sampleAnswer:
'You, however' (v. 14) — the primary contrast connector. Everything that follows is set against the backdrop of the deceivers in vv. 1-13. Timothy's path is defined over against theirs.
'And how from childhood…' (v. 15) — adds a second grounding for the command to continue: not only the trusted people who taught Timothy, but the long formation through Scripture from his earliest years.
'Which are able to give you wisdom for salvation' (v. 15) — a relative clause that unpacks the value of the holy scriptures Timothy has known since childhood. Scripture is not just familiar — it is powerfully salvific.
'Every scripture is inspired by God and useful for…' (v. 16) — shifts from Timothy's personal history to a universal claim. The 'and' here joins origin ('inspired by God') to function ('useful for…') — the two are inseparable: Scripture's usefulness flows from its divine origin.
'So that' (v. 17) — the purpose clause that links all of vv. 15-16 to the goal in v. 17. Everything Paul has said about Scripture's origin and usefulness serves this single end: the complete equipping of the person dedicated to God.
elements.questionHint: The purpose clause 'so that' in v. 17 is one of the most important words in the passage. What does it connect, and what does it tell you about Paul's ultimate concern — is it Scripture itself, or what Scripture produces?

elements.question: THEMES: How are the main ideas arranged in 2 Tim 3:14-17? Can you identify the major movements or sections in these verses?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Try to divide the passage into its main parts. What is Paul doing in v. 14? In v. 15? In vv. 16-17?
elements.sampleAnswer:
The passage moves through three clear sections:
1. The command and its personal grounding (v. 14): Continue — because of the trustworthy people who taught you. The appeal is to relationship and community as the basis for persisting in sound doctrine.
2. The salvific power of Scripture in Timothy's personal history (v. 15): From childhood Timothy has known the holy scriptures, which are able to give wisdom for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus. Scripture's primary role is pointing to Christ and the salvation he brings.
3. The universal claim about Scripture's nature and purpose (vv. 16-17): Every scripture is God-breathed and useful for a fourfold ministry of formation — and the goal of all this is the complete equipping of the person dedicated to God for every good work.
elements.questionHint: Try labelling each section with a single phrase: what is Paul doing in each part? Is he commanding? Grounding? Explaining? Justifying? What function does each section serve in the overall argument?

elements.question: OUTLINE: Having identified the 'big bones', write a simple structural outline of 2 Tim 3:14-17. What are the main sections and how would you label them?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Write your outline in 3 numbered points. Each point should capture one main section of the passage and explain what Paul is doing there…
elements.sampleAnswer:
1. The command, grounded in trusted people (v. 14): Continue in what you have learned and been convinced of — because you know the trustworthy people who taught you.
2. The personal foundation: Scripture that saves (v. 15): From childhood you have known the holy scriptures, which give wisdom for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus.
3. The universal foundation: Scripture that equips (vv. 16-17): Every scripture is God-breathed and useful for teaching, reproof, correction, and training in righteousness — so that the person dedicated to God may be completely equipped for every good work.
elements.questionHint: A good outline is simple enough to remember and preach from. Aim for 3 points. Notice that the passage gives two foundations for the command to continue (vv. 14-15 and vv. 16-17) — make sure your outline captures both and shows how they relate.

elements.text: Step 3: HEART — Identify the Message

elements.question: THEME: What is the main thing Paul is talking about in 2 Tim 3:14-17? State the theme of the passage as simply and clearly as you can — ideally in a short phrase.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Try to capture the theme in 3-6 words — the one subject this passage keeps coming back to…
elements.sampleAnswer: The theme is: continuing in Scripture as the God-breathed foundation for faithful ministry.
elements.questionHint: The theme is what the passage is about, before you say what it says about it. Is the passage primarily about Scripture itself — or about what Scripture enables? Try different formulations until you find one that captures the full scope of both the command and the foundation.

elements.question: MESSAGE: What is Paul saying about that theme? Complete this sentence: 'Paul tells Timothy that…' Aim for one or two clear sentences, without technical language, that even a child could understand.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Complete the sentence: 'Paul tells Timothy that…'
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul tells Timothy that he must keep going in what he has been taught — because the Scriptures he has known since childhood come from God himself, are powerful enough to lead people to salvation in Christ, and are fully equipped to prepare him for everything his ministry will require.
elements.questionHint: The message should complete the theme: the theme says 'what about?'; the message says 'what about it?' Try to hold together both the command ('continue') and the reason ('because Scripture is God-breathed and equipping'). Could a ten-year-old understand your sentence?

elements.text: Step 4: BRAIN — Understand the Purpose

elements.question: PURPOSE: Why is Paul making this point to Timothy? What is he trying to achieve by saying this, in this situation, at this moment?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what Timothy needs and what dangers Paul is guarding against. What would happen if Timothy did not hear this message?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul's purpose is twofold. First, he wants to prevent Timothy from drifting — from following the trajectory of the deceivers and charlatans who go from bad to worse (v. 13). The command to 'continue' is a bulwark against that drift. Second, he wants to equip Timothy for the long haul of ministry without Paul's physical presence. When Paul is gone, Timothy will need an anchor — something more permanent and reliable than any individual teacher. Paul's answer is Scripture: God-breathed, comprehensive, and fully sufficient to equip Timothy for every good work. Without this message, Timothy might either drift toward false teaching or feel unequipped to carry on. With it, he has both a command and the resources to obey it.
elements.questionHint: Think about what Timothy faces after Paul's death — the false teachers, the hostile world, the need to stand firm without the apostle's presence. What does he most need? And how does Paul's description of Scripture in vv. 15-17 provide exactly what Timothy needs for exactly that situation?

elements.question: CONNECTIONS: What connections do you see between the purpose of 2 Tim 3:14-17 and your own situation and congregation today? Capture your answer as a statement or question that brings the passage into the present.
elements.answerPlaceholder: What do you and your people need to hear from this passage? Why do they need to hear it now?
elements.sampleAnswer: A connecting question might be: 'In a world full of competing voices and distorted gospels, what is your anchor — and is it sufficient to equip you for everything your ministry will face?' Like Timothy, every church leader today faces the twin pressures of drift and inadequacy: the temptation to move away from what Scripture teaches in favour of what is culturally popular, and the feeling of being unequipped for the demands of ministry. The passage speaks directly to both: continue (against drift), and trust that the God-breathed Scriptures will equip you completely (against inadequacy). The challenge for many leaders is whether they truly believe that Scripture is sufficient — whether they go to it first, trust it deeply, and let it shape both their belief and their practice.
elements.questionHint: Think about the specific pressures in your context. Where are leaders in your church or community most tempted to drift from sound teaching? And where do they feel most unequipped? How does 2 Tim 3:14-17 speak to both of those specific pressures?

elements.text: Responding to 2 Timothy 3:14-17

elements.question: How did the message of 2 Tim 3:14-17 speak to you personally? What did you learn, or what did you need to be reminded of?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: How did the purpose of the passage help you to apply God's word to your own life? What change might this passage make to your thinking or living?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: In your own words, state the message you would share with others from this passage. What is the main thing you want people to understand?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: This passage is one of the great anchors of the BUILD curriculum — the foundation for the B in Biblical Understanding for In-service Leadership Development. Having now encountered it carefully, let it do what Paul says it was designed to do: give you wisdom, form you in righteousness, and equip you completely for every good work. 'Every scripture is inspired by God and useful for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, that the person dedicated to God may be capable and equipped for every good work.' (2 Tim 3:16-17)

This unit flows out of our encounter with 2 Timothy 3:14-17 and explores the nature and purpose of the Bible — and what that means for ministry in practice. It follows the ERA model of theological reflection introduced in Unit 9. The Action component focuses on personal Bible study and prayer.

elements.term: By the end of this unit you will be able to…
elements.question:
1. Repeat the process of theological reflection learnt in Unit 9
2. Show what 2 Timothy says about the nature and purpose of the Bible
3. Explain how the nature of the Bible relates to its purpose
4. Evaluate the relationship between private and public Bible study and prayer
5. Apply the lessons of Unit 10 to personal Bible study
6. Present a simple pattern of Bible study to others
elements.answer: Keep these outcomes in mind as you work through the unit. Return to them at the end to check your progress.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>Every scripture is inspired by God and useful for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, <sup>17</sup>that the person dedicated to God may be capable and equipped for every good work.

elements.text: The passage we have just encountered contains one of the most important statements in the Bible about the nature and purpose of Scripture. The letter B in BUILD stands for Biblical — and this unit takes what we have learnt about the Bible further, exploring it in greater depth through the ERA model: Encounter, Reflection, and Action.

elements.term: The three steps of theological reflection
elements.question: What are the three ERA steps, their definitions, activities, and outcomes?
elements.answer:
Step 1: ENCOUNTER something in God's world and God's word with a desire for wisdom.
Definition: To meet something new and surprising. Activity: Describe. Outcome: Discoveries.

Step 2: REFLECTION on those situations and the Scriptures in order to gain wisdom.
Definition: Pause for thought and go deeper. Activity: Consider. Outcome: Principles.

Step 3: ACTION in God's world with the wisdom that comes from God's word.
Definition: Do something new or different. Activity: Plan. Outcome: Practice.

elements.text: 11.2 Step 1: Encounter — Different Views of the Bible

The first step is to encounter something in God's world and God's word with a desire for wisdom. On this occasion we will first encounter our own ideas and those of others before turning to God's word. This is often easier — and it naturally prepares us to see what the Bible says by way of connection or contrast.

elements.question: Describe some of the things you hear people saying about the Bible — in churches and elsewhere in society. What attitudes to the Bible have you encountered?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about specific things people say — about whether the Bible is reliable, relevant, authoritative, or useful. Include a range of views, not just those you agree with…
elements.sampleAnswer: A wide range of attitudes exist. Within churches: some treat the Bible as a collection of inspiring stories but not as binding authority; others quote it selectively to support existing views; some read it mainly for personal comfort and ignore its harder teachings; others treat it as a magical text from which to extract predictions. Outside the church: many people assume the Bible is outdated, culturally irrelevant, or contradictory; some view it as a human document with no more authority than any other ancient text; others have a vague respect for it but have never read it. Even among leaders who preach from it regularly, there can be a gap between public proclamation of Scripture's authority and private uncertainty or neglect.
elements.questionHint: Think about conversations you have had or heard — in your church, your community, your family. What do people actually say when the Bible comes up? Try to describe a range of views rather than just the ones you agree with.

elements.question: What other sources of wisdom and insight do people around you turn to for direction or help in their lives and ministries — instead of, or alongside, the Bible?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the sources people actually consult — traditional authorities, personal experience, dreams, popular teaching, social media, community elders, and others…
elements.sampleAnswer: Common alternatives or supplements include: the wisdom of elders and ancestors, often treated as having equal or greater authority than Scripture in some cultural contexts; personal dreams and visions; popular Christian speakers and their books; prosperity teaching that selectively uses Scripture; social media content; traditional practices and rituals; political ideologies; and general cultural consensus. The question is not whether these sources have any value, but whether they are being evaluated in the light of Scripture or whether they are displacing it. Paul's concern in the Pastoral Epistles was precisely that false teachers were using alternative 'wisdom' to undermine the gospel — a pattern that recurs in every generation.
elements.questionHint: Think specifically about your own cultural and church context. What sources of authority do people treat as reliable guides for life and ministry? Which of these are checked against Scripture, and which are not?

elements.question: What do people mean when they claim that their ministry is 'biblical'? How would you describe the place the Bible actually has in your ministry and the ministry of those around you?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Be honest — is 'biblical' a claim that is carefully thought through, or is it sometimes used loosely? And how central is Scripture actually to your own ministry in practice?
elements.sampleAnswer: The word 'biblical' is often used as a badge of approval rather than a precise description. Many leaders call their ministry 'biblical' if they preach from the Bible occasionally, or if their teachings do not obviously contradict Scripture. But a truly biblical ministry goes further: it is shaped, structured, and evaluated by Scripture across all its dimensions — not just in preaching but in pastoral practice, leadership decisions, community life, and personal formation. The honest question is not 'do we use the Bible?' but 'does the Bible use us?' — does it have authority over us in areas that challenge or inconvenience us, not only in areas where we already agree with it?
elements.questionHint: Think about specific areas of your ministry — how leaders are chosen, how money is handled, how conflicts are resolved, how the poor are treated. In which areas does Scripture genuinely shape what you do? In which areas do other considerations take precedence?

elements.text: Encountering What God's Word Says About the Bible

elements.question: What do you think the Christians in Ephesus were saying about the Scriptures? Defend your answer with evidence from specific chapters and verses in the Pastoral Epistles.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look back through what you have studied in 1 and 2 Timothy. What evidence is there of how the Ephesian Christians — or the false teachers — were treating Scripture?
elements.sampleAnswer: The evidence from the Pastoral Epistles suggests that Scripture was being misused and distorted in Ephesus rather than straightforwardly rejected. The false teachers were occupying themselves with 'myths and endless genealogies' (1 Tim 1:3-4) — possibly drawing on Jewish traditions in ways that distorted the scriptural message. They wanted to be 'teachers of the law' (1 Tim 1:7) but did not understand what they were saying. In 2 Tim 3:6-8, Paul compares them to Jannes and Jambres who opposed Moses — people who claimed to handle God's word but actually resisted it. The false teachers in 2 Tim 2:17-18 were 'straying from the truth' by saying the resurrection had already occurred — a misreading of Scripture that undermined the gospel. The problem in Ephesus was not ignorance of Scripture but mishandling of it.
elements.questionHint: Look for passages where Paul mentions false teaching, myths, or distorted doctrine. What does the nature of the error tell you about how the false teachers were relating to Scripture? Were they ignoring it, or misusing it?

elements.question: What is striking about what 2 Timothy 3:14-17 and other parts of the Pastoral Epistles say about the Bible? What have you encountered in your studies so far about how these letters view Scripture?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Draw on your encounter work from Unit 10 and your wider reading of the Pastoral Epistles. What stands out about the way Paul talks about Scripture in these letters?
elements.sampleAnswer: Several things are striking. First, the strength of the claim in 2 Tim 3:16: 'every scripture is inspired by God' — this is not a modest claim but the strongest possible affirmation of Scripture's divine origin. Second, the comprehensiveness: 'every scripture', 'useful for' four functions, equipping for 'every good work' — there are no gaps. Third, Scripture's connection to salvation through Christ (2 Tim 3:15) — the Bible is not just a guide for living but the means through which people come to know the Saviour. Fourth, the repeated contrast between sound doctrine and false teaching throughout the letters — Paul treats faithfulness to Scripture as a life-or-death matter for the church, not a secondary concern. Fifth, the personal: Paul does not just make abstract claims about Scripture; he grounds it in Timothy's own story ('from childhood you have known the holy scriptures').
elements.questionHint: Think about what Paul does not say as well as what he does say. Does he qualify his claims about Scripture — suggesting it is only partially inspired, or useful only in some areas? What is the cumulative effect of everything the Pastoral Epistles say about the Bible?

elements.text: 11.3 Step 2: Reflection — The Nature and Purpose of the Bible

The second step is to reflect on those situations and the Scriptures in order to gain wisdom. We consider our discoveries and develop principles — in this case about the Bible and biblical ministry. We will do this under two headings drawn from 2 Timothy 3:16: the nature of the Bible ('All Scripture is…') and the purpose of the Bible ('…and is useful for…').

elements.question: Why might people hold the attitudes to the Bible that you described in section 11.2? What reasons — cultural, historical, personal, or theological — might lie behind those different views?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the roots of different attitudes to the Bible — what experiences, teachings, or cultural pressures might produce each of them?
elements.sampleAnswer: Attitudes to the Bible are shaped by many factors. Cultural pressure: in societies that value tradition, the Bible may be respected but not read carefully; in societies that value individualism, people may feel entitled to pick and choose what they accept. Painful experience: people who have had Scripture used against them — to justify oppression, abuse, or exclusion — may be understandably cautious about its authority. Poor teaching: many people have never been taught how to read the Bible well, so they either avoid it or misuse it. Prosperity distortion: where the prosperity gospel has taken hold, Scripture is read selectively for promises of blessing and healing, while passages about suffering and perseverance are marginalised. Pride: false teachers in every generation tend to use Scripture as a tool to advance their own agenda rather than submitting to its authority.
elements.questionHint: Think about a specific person you know who has a low view of Scripture — or who treats it selectively. What might have produced that attitude? Is it primarily intellectual, cultural, experiential, or relational? Understanding the roots helps us respond wisely.

elements.text: 11.3.1 The Nature of the Bible

The Bible has a dual nature: it is simultaneously God's words and human words. These are not in tension — they are held together in a wonderful unity. Because it is God's word, it bears his authority; because it is expressed through human words, it requires careful interpretation that takes historical, cultural, and literary context seriously.

elements.eyebrow: The Nature of the Bible — Five Principles
elements.term: What kind of book is the Bible?
elements.question: What are the five key principles about the nature of Scripture?
elements.answer:
a) Our Bible is God's words through people's words
'All Scripture is God-breathed…' — the words we read are God's words, expressed through human authors. God is the Bible's primary author, but he communicates through human authors writing in real historical settings. This means two things are equally true and must both be kept in mind:

Because it is people's words: each book has a particular historical setting and audience; we need to discover the original meaning for its audience; explanation is important.
Because it is God's words: it is relevant for all people in all places at all times; we must submit to its message and discover its relevance today; application is equally important.

b) Our Bible has been formed around Christ the Messiah
We trust the Bible because we trust Christ himself — he is our starting point. Christ trusted and repeatedly referred to the Old Testament Scriptures. He then predicted the formation of the New Testament through the writings of the Apostles, helped by the Holy Spirit (John 16:12-15). The Bible forms itself around Christ and the gospel as though they were a magnet.

c) Our Bible links the Scriptures with Salvation and the Saviour
The Bible is the words of God (Scriptures) about the word of God (the gospel) which is all about the Word of God (Christ, the Saviour). Scripture, Salvation, and the Saviour are inseparably linked — as illustrated in 2 Tim 3:15.

d) Our Bible links the Old Testament and the New Testament
Both Testaments belong together as a single story on the single theme of salvation, with the one hero — God in Christ. The Old Testament is a light shining ahead to Christ; the New Testament is a light shining back on Christ and the Old Testament. Each illuminates the other.

e) Our Bible is sufficient and complete
'All Scripture… every good work' (2 Tim 3:16-17) — the words 'all', 'useful', 'thoroughly', and 'every' signal completeness. Scripture is sufficient for salvation and for knowing the Saviour. Other books may be useful, but they are not essential in the way Scripture is.

elements.alt: Inverted triangle diagram illustrating 2 Timothy 3:15. At the top (widest point) the label reads 'you have known the holy Scriptures'. The middle narrows to 'which are able to make you wise for salvation'. The bottom point reads 'through faith in Christ Jesus'. The triangle narrows downward to show how Scripture leads through salvation to Christ.
elements.caption: 2 Timothy 3:15 — Scripture leads through salvation to Christ Jesus

elements.question: In your own words, explain what it means that the Bible is simultaneously God's words and human words. What practical difference does each side of this truth make to how we read and handle Scripture?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what the 'God's words' side requires of us — and what the 'human words' side requires. How do these two truths work together rather than against each other?
elements.sampleAnswer: That the Bible is God's words means it carries his authority — it is not merely human wisdom or ancient opinion that we are free to accept or reject. It is relevant for all people, in all places, at all times. It calls for submission, not just appreciation. That the Bible is human words means it was written by specific people in specific historical, cultural, and literary contexts — which we need to understand in order to hear what God was saying to its original audience, and therefore what he is saying to us. These two truths are not in tension. Understanding the human dimensions of a text does not undermine its divine authority — it actually helps us read it more faithfully. A text misread is a text misapplied, even if we believe it to be God's word. Handling Scripture well requires both humility before its divine authority and the effort of careful historical and literary understanding.
elements.questionHint: Think about a specific passage that is sometimes misapplied because people ignore its original context. What difference does taking the 'human words' side seriously make to how you interpret it? And think of a passage where people acknowledge its historical context but then treat it as if it has no relevance for today — what does the 'God's words' side require there?

elements.question: What does it mean to say that the Bible 'forms itself around Christ and the gospel as though they were a magnet'? How does this principle affect the way you read both the Old and New Testaments?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the relationship between Christ and the Old Testament — and how the New Testament illuminates the Old. How does reading both in the light of Christ change how you understand each?
elements.sampleAnswer: The magnet image captures the way Christ and the gospel are not just topics within the Bible but the centre around which everything else is organised. The Old Testament points forward to Christ — its laws, sacrifices, promises, and prophecies all find their fulfilment in him. The New Testament looks back at Christ — explaining what his life, death, and resurrection mean, and what they require of his people. This affects reading in both directions. Reading the Old Testament without reference to Christ produces either legalism (treating the law as a pathway to righteousness by works) or confusion (what do these ancient laws have to do with us?). Reading the New Testament without the Old loses the depth of its background — the promises, the covenants, the patterns that Christ fulfils. The whole Bible read with Christ at the centre becomes a coherent, magnificent story.
elements.questionHint: Try a specific example: how does reading Psalm 22 in the light of Christ's crucifixion change how you understand it? And how does reading the crucifixion accounts in the light of Psalm 22 deepen your understanding of what happened? This is the kind of mutual illumination the principle describes.

elements.text: 11.3.2 The Purpose of the Bible

The nature of Scripture leads directly to its purpose. Just as there are five points about the nature of Scripture (like five fingers on one hand), there are five corresponding points about its purpose (like five fingers on another hand). They are inseparably linked: what the Bible is determines what it does.

elements.term: How the nature of the Bible shapes its purpose in ministry
elements.question: How do the five aspects of Scripture's nature connect to its five purposes?
elements.answer:
a) It is God's words through people's words → It directs our preaching and teaching
Because Scripture is God's authoritative word expressed through human language, it is the primary content and standard for all Christian teaching and preaching.

b) It has Christ at the centre → It points people to Christ
Because the whole Bible is organised around Christ, faithful handling of any passage ultimately leads people toward him.

c) It is about salvation and leads to the Saviour → It leads people to be saved through Christ
Because Scripture's primary purpose is to give wisdom for salvation (2 Tim 3:15), faithful biblical ministry is inherently evangelistic — it leads people to saving faith in Christ.

d) It is formed around Christ and his gospel and teaches that gospel → It preserves the gospel and makes it central
Because the Bible guards and transmits the gospel, a ministry centred on Scripture is a ministry centred on the gospel. Biblical ministry and gospel-centred ministry are inseparable.

e) It is sufficient in every way → It equips leaders
Because Scripture is complete and sufficient — equipping the person dedicated to God for every good work (2 Tim 3:17) — it is the primary resource for leadership formation. Leaders who are formed by Scripture are equipped for everything their ministry will require.

elements.alt: Two-way arrow diagram showing the relationship between Biblical Ministry and Gospel Centred Ministry. A box on the left is labelled 'Biblical Ministry'. A box on the right is labelled 'Gospel Centred Ministry'. Two horizontal arrows connect them — one pointing right (from Biblical to Gospel Centred) and one pointing left (from Gospel Centred to Biblical) — showing that the two are mutually reinforcing and inseparable.
elements.caption: Biblical ministry and gospel-centred ministry are inseparable and mutually reinforcing

elements.question: In your own words, explain why biblical ministry and gospel-centred ministry are inseparable. What happens to a ministry that claims to be biblical but is not gospel-centred — or claims to be gospel-centred but drifts from Scripture?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what each term requires — and what each one loses without the other…
elements.sampleAnswer: They are inseparable because the Bible's central content is the gospel — and the gospel, rightly understood, always drives us back to Scripture. A ministry that claims to be biblical but is not gospel-centred will tend toward moralism: using Scripture as a rulebook rather than as the good news of what God has done in Christ. It may be technically accurate in its handling of texts but lifeless and joyless in its application. A ministry that claims to be gospel-centred but drifts from Scripture will tend toward sentimentalism or subjectivism: talking about grace and love without the hard edges and full demands of the gospel as Scripture actually presents it. Both distortions were present in Ephesus — and both are present in churches today. The corrective for both is the same: return to Scripture, read it faithfully, and let the gospel it contains shape everything.
elements.questionHint: Think of a real example of each distortion: a ministry that uses Scripture but is not gospel-centred, and a ministry that talks about the gospel but has drifted from Scripture. What goes wrong in each case — and what does it look like when both are held together well?

elements.text: 11.4 Step 3: Action — Personal Bible Study and Prayer

Paul tells Timothy that Scripture is useful — it is a practical book. But it must be used according to its nature and purpose. All five purposes we have identified — directing preaching, pointing to Christ, leading to salvation, preserving the gospel, equipping leaders — feed into the fifth: 'so that the person dedicated to God may be thoroughly equipped for every good work' (2 Tim 3:17).

This equipping begins with us. We must be equipped by the word if we are to equip others from the word — we need to watch our own 'life and doctrine closely' (1 Tim 4:16).

elements.question: Is Christian ministry a profession or a vocation? To what extent is your ministry a position you hold in the church — and how much is it about God's calling on your life? Where do you feel a sense of conflict between the two?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think honestly about your own experience of ministry. Where does it feel like a job — and where does it feel like a calling? What difference does that distinction make in practice?
elements.sampleAnswer: The distinction matters enormously. A professional approach to ministry treats it as a role with defined responsibilities, hours, and rewards — something you do between certain times and then stop. A vocational approach treats it as a calling that shapes the whole of life, not just the hours of 'official' ministry. The tension is real: church leaders often feel pressure to perform, to produce visible results, to meet institutional expectations. These pressures can crowd out the personal, relational, and spiritual dimensions of ministry. The corrective is not to abandon professionalism — preparation, accountability, and structure matter — but to ensure that the institutional dimensions of ministry are always in service of the vocational ones. Paul's concern for Timothy is not primarily about his performance but about his character, his faith, his relationship with Christ, and his personal engagement with Scripture.
elements.questionHint: Think about a week in your recent ministry. How much of your time was spent on things that feel more like administration than calling? And when did ministry feel most alive — most like what you were made for? What does that contrast tell you?

elements.question: It is easy for church leaders to be so busy feeding others from God's word that they starve themselves. Have you ever felt like that? How did it come to that point — and how have you been able to change the situation?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Be honest. When did you last encounter Scripture for your own nourishment — not for a sermon, not for a study, but simply to hear God speak to you?
elements.sampleAnswer: This is a very common experience among church leaders. The dynamics that produce it are predictable: preparation for teaching and preaching crowds out personal reading; the urgency of others' needs displaces one's own; over time, Scripture becomes associated primarily with work rather than with personal encounter with God. The result is a leader who can speak accurately about what God's word says but who has stopped being personally formed and fed by it. Recovery usually requires deliberate structural changes: protecting specific times for personal Scripture reading that are not preparation for anything else; using different methods (lectio divina, reading large portions aloud, journaling) to break the association with work; and accountability with others who will ask honestly whether personal feeding is happening.
elements.questionHint: Think about the last time you read Scripture and were genuinely surprised, moved, challenged, or comforted by something you hadn't noticed before. How long ago was that? What does your answer tell you about the health of your personal engagement with God's word?

elements.text: 11.4.1 Public and Private Bible Study and Prayer

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>First of all, then, I urge that requests, prayers, intercessions, and thanks be offered on behalf of all people, <sup>2</sup>even for kings and all those in authority, that we may lead a peaceful and quiet life in all godliness and dignity.

6By pointing out such things to the brothers and sisters, you will be a good servant of Christ Jesus, having nourished yourself on the words of the faith and of the good teaching that you have followed. 7But reject those myths fit only for the godless and gullible, and train yourself for godliness. 8For physical exercise has some value, but godliness is valuable in every way. It holds promise for the present life and for the life to come. 9This saying is trustworthy and deserves full acceptance. 10In fact this is why we work hard and struggle, because we have set our hope on the living God, who is the Savior of all people, and especially of those who believe.
11Command and teach these things. 12Let no one look down on you because you are young, but set an example for the believers in your speech, conduct, love, faithfulness, and purity. 13Until I come, give attention to the public reading of scripture, to exhortation, and to teaching. 14Do not neglect the spiritual gift you have, given to you and confirmed by prophetic words when the elders laid hands on you. 15Take pains with these things; be absorbed in them, so that everyone will see your progress. 16Be conscientious about how you live and what you teach. Persevere in this, because by doing so you will save both yourself and those who listen to you.

elements.question:
Skim-read the key passages from 1 and 2 Timothy listed below and fill in the four quadrants: public use of the Bible, public prayer, private use of the Bible, and private prayer. Note specific verses and the ideas they contain.

Key passages to read:
1 Tim 2:1-8 | 1 Tim 3:1-10 | 1 Tim 4:6-16
2 Tim 1:13-14 | 2 Tim 2:1-7, 14-15, 22-26 | 2 Tim 3:14-17 | 2 Tim 4:1-5
elements.answerPlaceholder:
PUBLIC USE OF THE BIBLE:
(e.g. 1 Tim 4:13)

PUBLIC PRAYER:
(e.g. 1 Tim 2:1-2, 8)

PRIVATE USE OF THE BIBLE:

PRIVATE PRAYER:
elements.sampleAnswer:
PUBLIC USE OF THE BIBLE:
1 Tim 4:13 — 'Give attention to the public reading of scripture, to exhortation, and to teaching.' Public Scripture reading is a primary ministry responsibility.
2 Tim 4:2 — 'Preach the message, be ready whether it is convenient or not.' Preaching is the public proclamation of God's word.
2 Tim 2:15 — 'Teaching the message of truth accurately' — the call to handle Scripture rightly in public ministry.
2 Tim 3:16-17 — Scripture is useful for teaching and training — with public instruction in view.

PUBLIC PRAYER:
1 Tim 2:1-2, 8 — Public prayer for all people, especially those in authority. Men are to pray 'in every place'.
1 Tim 3:4-5 — The overseer who leads his household well — implying leading in prayer.

PRIVATE USE OF THE BIBLE:
2 Tim 3:14-15 — 'Continue in the things you have learned and are confident about… from childhood you have known the holy scriptures.' Timothy's personal formation through Scripture.
1 Tim 4:6 — 'Having nourished yourself on the words of the faith' — private feeding on Scripture.
2 Tim 1:13 — 'Hold to the standard of sound words' — personal adherence to sound doctrine.

PRIVATE PRAYER:
2 Tim 1:3 — Paul gives thanks and prays for Timothy constantly, night and day — a model of personal, private prayer.
2 Tim 2:22 — 'Pursue righteousness, faith, love, and peace along with those who call on the Lord from a pure heart' — personal prayer and holiness.
1 Tim 4:7 — 'Train yourself for godliness' — the spiritual disciplines including prayer.
elements.questionHint: As you skim-read, look for explicit mentions of reading, teaching, preaching, and praying — and also for implicit references, where Paul describes practices that assume regular engagement with Scripture or prayer. Don't just look for obvious keywords.

elements.question: What we do publicly should be an expression of what we do privately. To what extent is that true of your own life? Where is there a gap between your public ministry of Scripture and prayer and your private engagement with both?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Be honest before God. Where is your public ministry flowing genuinely from private formation — and where might it be outrunning it?
elements.sampleAnswer: This is a question that requires honest self-examination rather than a 'correct' answer. The principle — that public ministry flows from private formation — is clear in Scripture: 'Nourish yourself on the words of the faith' (1 Tim 4:6) before pointing out those things to others. Jesus warns against the hypocrisy of those who perform publicly what they do not practise privately (Matt 6:5-15). The honest question is whether our public teaching, preaching, and prayer reflects a genuine private engagement with God — or whether we are drawing on reserves that are no longer being replenished. The health of our public ministry depends on the health of our private walk with God.
elements.questionHint: Think about the last time you preached or taught something that you were also personally wrestling with, learning from, or being challenged by. That is the kind of public ministry that flows from genuine private engagement. When was the last time that happened?

elements.text: 11.4.2 Studying the Bible According to the Nature of the Bible

elements.passageText: <sup>39</sup>You study the scriptures thoroughly because you think that in them you possess eternal life, and it is these same scriptures that testify about me, <sup>40</sup>but you are not willing to come to me so that you may have life.

elements.text: Because the Bible is shaped around Scripture, Salvation, and the Saviour, we need to study it 'with the grain' — in the way God has designed it to be studied. As you read the Bible for yourself, bear in mind three movements that follow the shape of 2 Timothy 3:15:

elements.alt: Inverted triangle diagram based on 2 Timothy 3:15 showing three steps of personal Bible study. At the top (widest point): '1. Know God's mind generally as you study the Scriptures'. The middle narrows to: '2. Apply the gospel and learn about salvation'. The bottom point reads: '3. Come to know your Saviour'. The triangle narrows downward, showing how Scripture study moves from general knowledge through salvation to personal knowledge of Christ.
elements.caption: Studying the Bible with the grain: Scripture → Salvation → Saviour

elements.question: Jesus warns the religious leaders that they study the Scriptures diligently but refuse to come to him for life (John 5:39-40). How can we make sure that our own study of Scripture leads to salvation in and through Jesus — rather than becoming an end in itself?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the difference between studying Scripture as a religious duty or academic exercise, and studying it as a means of knowing Christ. What habits and disciplines keep the latter alive?
elements.sampleAnswer: The religious leaders in John 5 knew the Scriptures thoroughly but refused to come to the one the Scriptures pointed to. Their study became self-referential — a source of status and identity rather than a pathway to Christ. Several things can prevent this drift. First, always ask: what does this passage tell me about who Christ is, what he has done, or what he calls me to? Second, move from information to application to encounter — not just 'what does this text say?' but 'what does this tell me about my own need of Christ, and how does it draw me to him?' Third, let Scripture regularly drive you to prayer — addressing God in the light of what you have just read rather than simply closing the book and moving on. Fourth, keep the three movements of 2 Tim 3:15 in mind: Scripture → Salvation → Saviour. Every passage, rightly read, moves in that direction.
elements.questionHint: Think about the difference between reading Scripture and meeting God. They are not automatically the same thing — as the religious leaders in John 5 demonstrate. What practices and attitudes ensure that your reading of Scripture is also an encounter with the living Christ?

elements.text: 11.4.3 A Pattern for Personal Bible Study

The principles we have learnt for encountering the Bible apply to personal Bible study too. Here is a simple daily pattern for approaching any passage of Scripture — using the word GOSPEL to help us remember it. It is a way of preaching the gospel to ourselves day by day.

elements.eyebrow: The GOSPEL Pattern for Personal Bible Study
elements.term: A daily framework for meeting God in Scripture
elements.question: What are the six steps of the GOSPEL pattern for personal Bible study?
elements.answer:
G — God
As you meditate on the passage, look first for what it teaches about God. Focus particularly on his character, beginning with his might and majesty.

O — Ourselves
The Bible also tells us the truth about ourselves — it is a mirror in which we see our own brokenness. Look for ways in which the passage shows up your sinfulness, as well as any signs of God's love for you.

S — Salvation
Turn to note anything that points to salvation in Christ Jesus — truths that lead you to Christ's saving work on the cross.

P — Promise
There may be indicators of the promise of God's Holy Spirit and his grace and gifts for us today. Truths about the work of the promised Holy Spirit can bring assurance of all that God has done for us.

E — Eternity
The passage may point to eternity, to the life of the world to come, or to Christ's return and his future judgement. If it does, take that perspective into your day.

L — Life
Finally, apply all this to your life today. Look for commands to obey, examples to follow, warnings to heed, or promises to hold on to. Apply the message to your hopes and fears for the day.

Practical tips: Use lectionary readings for a balanced diet. Use a notebook to concentrate and develop ideas. Write down and memorise a key verse to carry into the day.

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>For we too were once foolish, disobedient, misled, enslaved to various passions and desires, spending our lives in evil and envy, hateful and hating one another. <sup>4</sup>But when the kindness of God our Savior and his love for humanity appeared, <sup>5</sup>he saved us not because of any righteous deeds we did but because of his mercy, through the washing of new birth and the renewing of the Holy Spirit, <sup>6</sup>whom he poured out on us in full measure through Jesus Christ our Savior. <sup>7</sup>And so, since we have been justified by his grace, we become heirs with the confident expectation of eternal life. <sup>8</sup>This saying is trustworthy, and I want you to insist on these things, so that those who have believed in God may be careful to engage in good works. These things are good and beneficial for all people.

elements.question:
Practise the GOSPEL pattern now using Titus 3:3-8. Work through each letter in turn and write what you find under each heading.

G — What does the passage teach about God?
O — What does it reveal about ourselves?
S — What does it say about salvation?
P — What promises of the Spirit or grace does it contain?
E — What does it say about eternity?
L — How does it apply to your life today?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Work through each letter in turn. Take your time — this is a practice run for a daily habit…
elements.sampleAnswer:
G — God: The passage reveals God as Saviour (vv. 4, 6), characterised by kindness and love for humanity (v. 4), mercy (v. 5), and grace (v. 7). He is a God who acts — who pours out the Holy Spirit in full measure (v. 6) and justifies those who do not deserve it.

O — Ourselves: Verse 3 is a mirror held up to human nature apart from God: foolish, disobedient, misled, enslaved, hateful, and hating one another. This is not a flattering picture — and Paul says 'we too were once' all of this. There is no room for pride.

S — Salvation: The passage is saturated with salvation language: saved (v. 5), washing of new birth, renewing of the Holy Spirit (v. 5), justified by grace (v. 7). Salvation is entirely God's work — 'not because of any righteous deeds we did but because of his mercy'.

P — Promise: The Holy Spirit is promised and given in full measure through Jesus Christ (v. 6). The gift of the Spirit is the present reality of salvation — new birth and renewal are his work.

E — Eternity: We become heirs 'with the confident expectation of eternal life' (v. 7). The horizon of the passage extends beyond the present into the inheritance that awaits.

L — Life: Those who have received this salvation are to 'be careful to engage in good works' (v. 8) — not to earn salvation but as the natural response to it. The application is practical: what good works is God calling me to today, in response to all he has done for me?
elements.questionHint: Don't rush through the letters — each one is a separate encounter with the passage. After you have worked through all six, notice: did any one letter open up something you would have missed if you had just read the passage quickly?

elements.text: 11.4.4 Praying from the Word

elements.term: A simple framework for praying from Scripture
elements.question: What are the four movements of the ACTS prayer pattern?
elements.answer:
A — Adoration
Turn what you have learnt about the Lord into praise. Begin with who God is — his character, his greatness, his love — before moving to what you need from him.

C — Confession
That will naturally lead to the confession of sin. As we see God more clearly and ourselves more honestly (from the O step of GOSPEL), confession flows naturally.

T — Thanksgiving
First for your salvation — preach the gospel to yourself as you give thanks. Then for the many other blessings God has given you.

S — Supplication
'Supplication' means to ask for something. End by laying your requests before God — for yourself, for others, for your church, for the world.

There is no better moment to pray than as you study the Bible or immediately after. As God speaks to you through his word, respond in prayer.

elements.question: Using the ACTS pattern, pray through what you have discovered from your study of Titus 3:3-8 and this unit as a whole. Write out your prayer, or use the ACTS headings to structure a time of prayer now.
elements.answerPlaceholder:
Adoration:

Confession:

Thanksgiving:

Supplication:

elements.text: 11.6 Extra: The BUILD Modules and the Flow of the Bible

One practical way in which the shape of the Bible is used in BUILD is in shaping its curriculum. The ten modules follow the flow of the Bible — illustrated using the key books each module is based on: 2 Timothy (Module 1), Genesis (2), Mark (3), Nehemiah (4), Acts (5), Psalms (6), Ephesians (7), Proverbs (8), Isaiah (9), Revelation (10).

elements.alt: Diagram showing the flow of the Bible as reflected in the BUILD modules. The ten key books are arranged in canonical order to show how the BUILD curriculum moves through the broad sweep of the biblical story: 2 Timothy, Genesis, Mark, Nehemiah, Acts, Psalms, Ephesians, Proverbs, Isaiah, Revelation.
elements.caption: The BUILD modules and the flow of the Bible

elements.question: What do you notice about the arrangement of the ten BUILD modules? What might the ordering achieve — and are there any books that seem to break the pattern or need explaining?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at the list of ten books. What order are they in — and what does that tell you about how the BUILD curriculum is designed to move through the Bible?
elements.sampleAnswer: The most immediately obvious feature is that Module 1 begins with 2 Timothy — which is a New Testament letter, not the beginning of the Bible. This is deliberate: 2 Timothy is chosen as the entry point because it addresses the nature of the gospel, Scripture, and leadership directly — the foundations the whole curriculum is built on. After that, the modules broadly follow the canonical flow of the Bible: Genesis (the beginning of the OT story), moving through the historical books (Nehemiah), the Gospels (Mark), Acts (the NT story), poetry (Psalms), the epistles (Ephesians), wisdom (Proverbs), the prophets (Isaiah), and finally Revelation (the end of the biblical story). The arrangement ensures that BUILD participants encounter the whole sweep of the Bible's story across the ten modules — not just a selection of favourite passages.
elements.questionHint: Notice that Module 1 is 2 Timothy — a New Testament letter that comes near the end of the canon. Why might the curriculum begin there rather than with Genesis? And then look at the broad sweep from Module 2 to Module 10 — what pattern do you see in the order of the books?

elements.text: This unit has taken us from encounter (what people say and do with the Bible) through reflection (the nature and purpose of Scripture) to action (personal Bible study and prayer). The principles are clear — but principles only become practice through the discipline of daily return to God's word. May the GOSPEL and ACTS patterns become a living rhythm in your ministry — and may you 'nourish yourself on the words of the faith' (1 Tim 4:6) as faithfully as you feed others from them.

This unit applies the four steps from Unit 4 to 2 Timothy 4:1-5 — a particularly important passage in 2 Timothy. By now you should be able to work more independently through the four steps, and to identify the parts of the process where you need more help. Keep referring back to Unit 4 as you go.

elements.term: Refer back to Unit 4 for the full learning outline.
elements.question: By the end of this unit you will be able to…
elements.answer:
1. Follow and apply the four steps of Encountering a Bible Passage to 2 Timothy 4:1-5
2. Share the message of 2 Timothy 4:1-5 with a group of people today

elements.text: The Passage: 2 Timothy 4:1-5

Before you begin the four steps, read the passage carefully — more than once, and aloud if you can. This passage has already appeared in Unit 2, where the phrase 'I give you this charge' was used to illustrate how context shapes meaning. Now you encounter the full passage. Notice the weight of the opening: Paul issues this charge in the presence of God and of Christ Jesus, who will judge the living and the dead. Let the solemnity of that framing shape how you read everything that follows.

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>I solemnly charge you before God and Christ Jesus, who will judge the living and the dead, and by his appearing and his kingdom: <sup>2</sup>preach the message, be ready whether it is convenient or not, reprove, rebuke, exhort with complete patience and instruction. <sup>3</sup>For there will be a time when people will not tolerate sound teaching. Instead, following their own desires, they will accumulate teachers for themselves, because they have an insatiable curiosity to hear new things. <sup>4</sup>And they will turn away from hearing the truth, but will turn aside to myths. <sup>5</sup>You, however, be self-controlled in all things, endure hardship, do the work of an evangelist, fulfill your ministry.

elements.text: Step 1: FLESH — Study the Details
1a) Set the Text in Context

elements.passageText:
3:14You, however, must continue in the things you have learned and are confident about.
