Both connect the gospel to visible, godly conduct in every area of community life (Titus 2:10b — 'to bring credit to the teaching about God our Saviour'; 3:1-2, 8, 14 — the call to good works; cf. 1 Tim 2:2; 3:15; 6:1). The shared conviction is that the gospel produces a community whose life reinforces rather than undermines the message it proclaims.
elements.questionHint: Try to identify 3-4 themes that appear in both letters. For each one, find a verse in Titus and a corresponding verse in 1 Timothy. What does the overlap tell you about the shared concerns of both letters?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>From Paul, a slave of God and apostle of Jesus Christ, to further the faith of God's chosen ones and the knowledge of the truth that is in keeping with godliness, <sup>2</sup>in hope of eternal life, which God, who does not lie, promised before time began. <sup>3</sup>But now in his own time he has made his message evident through the preaching I was entrusted with according to the command of God our Savior.

<sup>11</sup>For the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all people. <sup>12</sup>It trains us to reject godless ways and worldly desires and to live self-controlled, upright, and godly lives in the present age, <sup>13</sup>as we wait for the happy fulfillment of our hope in the glorious appearing of our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ. <sup>14</sup>He gave himself for us to set us free from every kind of lawlessness and to purify for himself a people who are his very own, who are eager to do good.

elements.question: DIFFERENCES FROM 1 TIMOTHY — CONTENT: Looking at Titus 1:1-3 and 2:11-14, what content does Titus develop that is less prominent in 1 Timothy? What is distinctive about Titus's theological emphasis?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at the opening of Titus (1:1-3) and the gospel summary in 2:11-14. What theological themes receive more sustained or distinctive treatment in Titus than in 1 Timothy?
elements.sampleAnswer: Titus has a richer and more developed theological opening than 1 Timothy. Titus 1:1-3 traces the gospel from God's eternal purpose ('promised before time began') through its present revelation ('but now in his own time he has made his message evident') — giving the letter a stronger salvation-historical foundation from the very start. The gospel summaries in Titus are also particularly rich: 2:11-14 presents grace as something that has 'appeared' (an almost incarnational term) and that trains us to live godly lives as we wait for Christ's return — a past, present, and future shape to the gospel that is more sustained here than in 1 Timothy. Titus also has the magnificent gospel summary in 3:3-7, with its emphasis on mercy, washing, renewal, and the gift of the Spirit. These passages develop the theological foundations of the gospel more expansively than their counterparts in 1 Timothy.
elements.questionHint: Compare the openings of 1 Timothy and Titus — what does each emphasise in the first three verses? Then look at 2:11-14 in Titus alongside 1 Tim 2:3-7. What does Titus develop that 1 Timothy states more briefly?

elements.text: 14.2 Understanding the Pastoral Epistles as a Whole

Having now looked at the background of all three Pastoral Epistles — 1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, and Titus — we can step back and see how the three letters relate to each other. They were most likely written in this order: 1 Timothy first, then Titus, then 2 Timothy.

elements.eyebrow: The Relationship Between the Three Pastoral Epistles
elements.term: 1 Timothy → Titus → 2 Timothy
elements.question: How do the three Pastoral Epistles relate to each other — in order, urgency, and connection?
elements.answer:
1 Timothy (probably first)
From Paul, who is free to travel (in Macedonia, 1:3), to Timothy in Ephesus. False teachers have already emerged from within the church leadership, established themselves, and begun to destroy the church. Being a true teacher of the gospel — stopping the false teachers and appointing other true teachers — is the essential task. Urgent and important.

Titus (probably second)
From Paul, who is still free (planning to winter at Nicopolis, 3:12), to Titus on Crete. False teachers are already present but the problems are less severe. Being a true teacher of the gospel and appointing other true teachers is still vital — but the situation is still being established rather than already in crisis. Titus seems to be based on 1 Timothy but written for a different setting. Important but less urgent.

2 Timothy (probably third)
From Paul, who is imprisoned in Rome and facing death, to Timothy in Ephesus. Being a true teacher of the gospel and passing gospel ministry on to other true teachers is now more important and urgent than ever — both so that Timothy can come to Paul's side, and so that true gospel ministry will continue after Paul's death. Extremely urgent and extremely important.

The connections: There is a direct connection between 1 Timothy and Titus (Paul uses similar material for a different setting) and between 1 Timothy and 2 Timothy (Paul builds on 1 Timothy in a worsening situation). The connection between 2 Timothy and Titus is indirect — they both relate to similar issues and both arose from 1 Timothy, but Paul is writing to different people in different places.

elements.alt: Diagram showing the relationship between the three Pastoral Epistles. Three boxes are connected: '1 Timothy — From a free Paul to Timothy in Ephesus — urgent and important' is connected by solid lines to both 'Titus — From a free Paul to Titus on Crete — important but less urgent' and '2 Timothy — From Paul in prison to Timothy in Ephesus — very urgent and very important'. Titus and 2 Timothy are connected by a dashed line showing an indirect relationship. The order reads: Titus on the left, 1 Timothy in the centre, 2 Timothy on the right.
elements.caption: The relationship between the three Pastoral Epistles: order, urgency, and connections

elements.question: Having seen all three Pastoral Epistles together, what strikes you most about the progression from 1 Timothy through Titus to 2 Timothy? What does the increasing urgency tell us about the nature of gospel ministry and the importance of faithful leaders?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the trajectory: from 'urgent and important' to 'important but less urgent' to 'extremely urgent and extremely important'. What does that pattern reveal?
elements.sampleAnswer: The progression is striking and sobering. The same basic concerns — true gospel teaching, qualified leadership, godly community life — appear in all three letters. But the stakes rise dramatically from letter to letter. In 1 Timothy the crisis is urgent; in Titus it is being addressed before it becomes critical; in 2 Timothy it has become existential — Paul is about to die, and the question is whether the gospel will survive in faithful hands. This progression underlines that the concerns are not merely institutional (who runs the church?) but gospel-critical (will the truth be preserved and passed on?). The increasing urgency also shows that faithful leaders are not optional extras in the life of the church — they are the means by which God ensures the gospel continues from one generation to the next. The stakes of leadership formation are eternal.
elements.questionHint: Think about why Paul keeps addressing the same themes (sound doctrine, qualified leaders, godly conduct) in three different letters. Does repetition signal unimportance or urgency? And what does the fact that these concerns intensify rather than resolve tell you about the nature of gospel ministry?

elements.text: 14.3 The Passage: Titus 1:5-9

Before you begin the four steps, read the passage carefully — more than once, and aloud if you can. This passage lists the qualifications for elders in the churches on Crete. As you read, notice how the list moves from family and personal character (vv. 6-8) to doctrinal fidelity (v. 9) — and how v. 9 is the climax and the key to everything that precedes it.

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>The reason I left you in Crete was to set in order the things that were left unfinished and to appoint elders in every town, as I directed you. <sup>6</sup>An elder must be blameless, the husband of one wife, with faithful children who cannot be charged with dissipation or rebellion. <sup>7</sup>For the overseer must be blameless as one entrusted with God's work, not arrogant, not prone to anger, not addicted to wine, not violent, not greedy for gain. <sup>8</sup>Instead he must be hospitable, devoted to what is good, sensible, upright, devout, and self-controlled. <sup>9</sup>He must hold firmly to the faithful message as it has been taught, so that he will be able to give exhortation in such healthy teaching and correct those who speak against it.

elements.text: Step 1: FLESH — Study the Details
1a) Set the Text in Context

elements.passageText:
1From Paul, a slave of God and apostle of Jesus Christ, to further the faith of God's chosen ones and the knowledge of the truth that is in keeping with godliness, 2in hope of eternal life, which God, who does not lie, promised before time began. 3But now in his own time he has made his message evident through the preaching I was entrusted with according to the command of God our Savior.
4To Titus, my genuine son in a common faith. Grace and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Savior.
5The reason I left you in Crete was to set in order the things that were left unfinished and to appoint elders in every town, as I directed you. 6An elder must be blameless, the husband of one wife, with faithful children who cannot be charged with dissipation or rebellion. 7For the overseer must be blameless as one entrusted with God's work, not arrogant, not prone to anger, not addicted to wine, not violent, not greedy for gain. 8Instead he must be hospitable, devoted to what is good, sensible, upright, devout, and self-controlled. 9He must hold firmly to the faithful message as it has been taught, so that he will be able to give exhortation in such healthy teaching and correct those who speak against it.
10For there are many rebellious people, idle talkers, and deceivers, especially those with Jewish connections, 11who must be silenced because they are upsetting whole families by teaching what they should not teach for the sake of dishonest gain. 12A Cretan prophet of their own said, “Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, lazy gluttons.” 13Such testimony is true. For this reason rebuke them sharply so that they will be healthy in the faith 14and not pay attention to Jewish myths and commands of people who reject the truth. 15To the pure, all things are pure, but to those who are corrupt and unbelieving, nothing is pure, but both their minds and consciences are corrupted. 16They profess to know God but with their deeds they deny him, since they are detestable, disobedient, and unfit for any good work.

elements.question: STORY: Where does Titus 1:5-9 come in the argument of the letter? What comes immediately before it (vv. 1-4) and after it (vv. 10-16), and how does that context shape the significance of the elder qualifications?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at what the letter opens with (vv. 1-4), then what immediately follows the elder qualifications (vv. 10-16). Why are qualified elders needed at this particular moment in the letter's argument?
elements.sampleAnswer: The letter opens with a rich gospel foundation (vv. 1-3): Paul's apostleship is in service of the faith, knowledge, and hope rooted in God's eternal promise now revealed through the gospel. This sets the theological stakes before anything practical is addressed. Then comes the personal greeting to Titus (v. 4) and immediately the commission to appoint elders (v. 5). The qualifications for elders (vv. 5-9) are not given in a vacuum — they are flanked on one side by the gospel (vv. 1-3) and on the other by the description of false teachers (vv. 10-16). This positioning is crucial: the reason qualified elders are needed is precisely the threat described in vv. 10-16 — rebellious people, idle talkers, and deceivers who are 'upsetting whole families' and 'professing to know God but denying him with their deeds.' The elder qualifications are the answer to the false teacher problem. Faithful leaders who hold firmly to sound doctrine (v. 9) are the God-given protection against those who undermine it.
elements.questionHint: Notice that the elder qualifications (vv. 5-9) lead directly into the description of false teachers (vv. 10-16). What does that connection tell you about why the qualifications are structured the way they are — especially v. 9? What would happen to the churches on Crete if unqualified elders were appointed?

elements.question: SITUATION: What do you notice about Titus 1:5-9 in relation to the situation in Titus — the people, problems, and places? How does the specific context of Crete shape the urgency and content of the elder qualifications?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about what you know of the Cretan situation — incomplete church organisation, false teachers, a particular cultural context. How does that situation make these qualifications both urgent and specific?
elements.sampleAnswer: The situation shapes the passage directly. Titus is in Crete — a setting with its own cultural challenges (v. 12 quotes a Cretan proverb about dishonesty and laziness) and with churches that are still being organised ('set in order the things that were left unfinished', v. 5). The false teachers are already active (vv. 10-11), upsetting families and teaching for dishonest gain. In that context, the elder qualifications are not abstract ideals — they are the specific antidote to specific problems. The emphasis on 'blameless' (vv. 6, 7), on 'not greedy for gain' (v. 7 — directly countering those who teach 'for the sake of dishonest gain' in v. 11), and on holding firmly to sound doctrine so as to 'correct those who speak against it' (v. 9) are all shaped by the precise threats the Cretan churches face.
elements.questionHint: Look at the list of negative qualities in v. 7 ('not arrogant, not prone to anger, not addicted to wine, not violent, not greedy for gain') and compare them with the description of the false teachers in vv. 10-16. Are there any echoes? What does that connection tell you about why these specific qualities matter?

elements.question: STYLE: What features of Titus 1:5-9 show that it is part of a personal letter? What do you notice about Paul's tone and the way he structures the elder qualifications?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the personal commission Paul gives Titus, the list format, the movement from positive to negative to positive, and the climax in v. 9. What kind of writing is this — and how does it serve its purpose?
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage is unmistakably a personal letter in its opening (v. 5 refers to a previous personal conversation: 'as I directed you'). Paul gives Titus a specific commission drawn from their shared history together. The list of qualifications in vv. 6-9 uses a form common in the ancient world for describing qualified leaders — but Paul adapts it for a specifically Christian and gospel purpose. The structure moves from the elder's family and personal character (v. 6) through the negative qualities to avoid (v. 7) to the positive qualities to cultivate (v. 8), culminating in the doctrinal requirement of v. 9. This climactic structure is deliberate: the list is not simply a checklist of virtues but a progression that leads to the essential qualification — holding firmly to the faithful message. Character without doctrinal fidelity is insufficient; the elder must be both personally and doctrinally trustworthy.
elements.questionHint: Notice that vv. 6-8 deal with character and conduct, while v. 9 deals with doctrine. Why does Paul put doctrine last? Is the last item in a list typically the least important or the most important? What does that structural choice tell you about what Paul considers the essential qualification?

elements.text: 1b) See the Details of the Text

Read Titus 1:5-9 again carefully. Look for: repeated and related words and phrases; important, complicated, or unusual words and phrases; and connections and links — including the repeated word 'blameless', the 'for' connecting vv. 6 and 7, the contrast between 'not' (v. 7) and 'instead' (v. 8), and the purpose clause in v. 9 ('so that').

elements.question: LIST the important words, phrases, and relationships you notice in Titus 1:5-9. What stands out as you read carefully?
elements.answerPlaceholder: List words and phrases — one per line if that helps. Don't explain them yet, just notice and list them…
elements.sampleAnswer:
'Set in order… appoint elders in every town' (v. 5 — the commission; comprehensive in scope)
'As I directed you' (v. 5 — a prior personal commission; Titus already knows this)
'Blameless' (vv. 6 and 7 — repeated; the overarching character requirement)
'The husband of one wife… faithful children' (v. 6 — the elder's family as evidence of character)
'For' (v. 7 — connects vv. 6 and 7; the 'overseer' is the same person as the 'elder')
'Entrusted with God's work' (v. 7 — the elder as a steward, not an owner)
'Not… not… not… not… not' (v. 7 — five negatives; what to avoid)
'Instead' (v. 8 — sharp contrast; pivots from negatives to positives)
'Hospitable, devoted to what is good, sensible, upright, devout, self-controlled' (v. 8 — six positives)
'Hold firmly to the faithful message as it has been taught' (v. 9 — the doctrinal requirement)
'So that' (v. 9 — purpose clause; the goal of doctrinal fidelity)
'Give exhortation in such healthy teaching' (v. 9 — positive purpose)
'Correct those who speak against it' (v. 9 — negative purpose)
'Healthy teaching' / 'faithful message' (v. 9 — the gospel as a standard)
elements.questionHint: Try reading the passage three times: once for repeated words, once for the list structure (what kinds of things are being listed?), and once for the purpose clause in v. 9. What is the climax of the passage, and how does the structure lead you there?

elements.text: 1c) Seek the Meaning from the Details

elements.question: EXPLAIN the words, phrases, and relationships you listed. What do they mean in their context — both in today's language and in the original setting? What is their significance in the argument Paul is building?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Work through your list from the previous question. For each word or phrase: what does it mean, and why does it matter here?
elements.sampleAnswer:
'Set in order… appoint elders in every town' — the scope is comprehensive: not just the main town but every town on Crete. The churches need qualified leadership everywhere, not just in the centre.
'Blameless' (vv. 6 and 7) — this does not mean sinless perfection but rather a life that is above legitimate public criticism. The elder's life must be transparent enough that no serious charge can be credibly made against it.
'The husband of one wife' — probably refers to marital faithfulness rather than simply being married once; the elder must be completely devoted to his spouse.
'Faithful children who cannot be charged with dissipation or rebellion' — the elder's household is a visible demonstration of his leadership ability. If he cannot lead his family well, how can he lead the church?
'Entrusted with God's work' (v. 7) — 'God's household' is what the elder manages; he is a steward, not an owner. This raises the stakes: he is accountable to God for how he handles what is not his.
'Not… not… not… not… not' (v. 7) — the five negatives describe the characteristics that would make an elder unfit for this stewardship: arrogance, anger, drunkenness, violence, and greed.
'Instead' (v. 8) — the pivot from what to avoid to what to cultivate. The six positive qualities are not a separate list but the positive mirror image of the negatives.
'Hold firmly to the faithful message as it has been taught' (v. 9) — 'the faithful message' is the gospel. The elder must not only know it but hold firmly to it — under pressure to drift or compromise.
'So that' (v. 9) — the purpose clause reveals why doctrinal fidelity matters: not merely as an intellectual exercise but as an equipping for ministry — to encourage with sound doctrine and to refute those who contradict it. The elder's character (vv. 6-8) enables the credibility of his ministry; his doctrinal fidelity (v. 9) defines its content.
elements.questionHint: For the key phrase 'hold firmly to the faithful message as it has been taught' (v. 9), ask: what does 'hold firmly' imply about the pressure to let go? And what does 'as it has been taught' add — is the elder free to develop his own version of the message, or is he accountable to something received?

elements.text: 1d) Summarise Your Findings

elements.question: Drawing on your work so far, summarise your main findings from Step 1. What are the most important ideas you want to carry forward into the next steps?
elements.answerPlaceholder: What are the two or three most important things you have discovered about this passage so far?
elements.sampleAnswer:
Three main ideas emerge:
1. The elder is a steward, not an owner — he is 'entrusted with God's work' (v. 7). His character requirements flow from this stewardship: he must be trustworthy in every dimension of his life because he is handling what belongs to God.
2. Character and doctrine belong together — the qualifications move from personal and family character (vv. 6-8) to doctrinal fidelity (v. 9). Neither is sufficient without the other: a doctrinally sound leader with a disordered personal life lacks credibility; a personally admirable leader who cannot hold firmly to sound doctrine cannot fulfil the essential functions of his role.
3. Doctrinal fidelity is not passive but active — the purpose clause in v. 9 shows that holding firmly to the gospel is for the sake of two things: encouraging believers with sound teaching and correcting those who contradict it. The qualified elder is equipped for both defence and offence in the ministry of the gospel.
elements.questionHint: If you had to name the two or three most important things this passage is saying — things you could carry into Steps 2-4 as a foundation — what would they be? Make each one a complete thought rather than a single word.

elements.text: Step 2: SKELETON — Outline the Structure

elements.question: LINKS: What important linking words contribute to the structure of Titus 1:5-9? How do they hold the argument together and show the logic of what Paul is saying?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Look at 'the reason' (v. 5), 'for' (v. 7), 'instead' (v. 8), 'so that' (v. 9). What does each link connect, and how does that shape the overall structure?
elements.sampleAnswer:
'The reason I left you in Crete was…' (v. 5) — grounds the commission in Paul's personal relationship with Titus and their shared history. The instruction is not arbitrary but flows from a specific, purposeful deployment.
'For' (v. 7) — connects the 'elder' in v. 6 with the 'overseer' in v. 7, confirming they are the same person. It also introduces the theological grounding: the overseer must be blameless because he is 'entrusted with God's work.'
'Not… not… not… not… not' / 'Instead' (vv. 7-8) — the contrast structure is the backbone of the middle section. The negatives define what an elder must not be; 'instead' pivots to what he must be.
'So that' (v. 9) — the purpose clause is the structural climax. Everything in vv. 5-9 has been leading to this: the point of the elder's character and doctrinal fidelity is the ministry it enables — encouraging with sound doctrine and correcting those who oppose it.
elements.questionHint: The 'so that' in v. 9 is one of the most important words in the passage. What does it connect, and what does it tell you about Paul's ultimate concern — is it the elder's character, or what that character enables him to do?

elements.question: THEMES: How are the main ideas arranged in Titus 1:5-9? Can you identify the major movements or sections in these verses?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Try to divide the passage into its main parts. What is Paul doing in v. 5? In vv. 6-8? In v. 9?
elements.sampleAnswer:
The passage falls into three clear movements:
1. The commission (v. 5): Titus is to complete the unfinished work and appoint elders in every town — the task is specific, comprehensive, and urgent.
2. The character qualifications (vv. 6-8): Moving from family life (v. 6) through the negative qualities to avoid (v. 7) to the positive qualities to cultivate (v. 8). The elder's whole life must reflect the trustworthiness required of a steward of God's household.
3. The doctrinal requirement and its purpose (v. 9): The climax — the elder must hold firmly to the faithful message so that he can encourage with sound teaching and correct those who oppose it. Doctrinal fidelity is not one item among many; it is the essential purpose-defining qualification.
elements.questionHint: Try labelling each section with a single phrase: what is Paul doing in each part — commissioning, qualifying, or purposing? And notice how the passage moves from the general (appoint elders everywhere) to the specific (here is what they must be like) to the purposive (here is why it matters).

elements.question: OUTLINE: Having identified the 'big bones', write a simple structural outline of Titus 1:5-9. What are the main sections and how would you label them?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Write your outline in 3 numbered points. Each point should capture one main section and explain what Paul is doing there…
elements.sampleAnswer:
1. The commission: appoint elders in every town (v. 5) — the task is comprehensive and urgent; every church needs qualified leadership.
2. The character qualifications: what an elder must be (vv. 6-8) — blameless in family life, avoiding the vices that undermine credibility, cultivating the virtues that build trust. The elder is a steward of God's household and must be trustworthy in every dimension.
3. The doctrinal requirement and its purpose: hold firmly to the faithful message (v. 9) — so that the elder can encourage believers with sound teaching and correct those who oppose it. Doctrinal fidelity is the capstone qualification because it defines the purpose of everything else.
elements.questionHint: A good outline is simple enough to remember and preach from. Aim for 3 points. Make sure each point captures not just what Paul says but what he is doing — commissioning, describing, purposing.

elements.text: Step 3: HEART — Identify the Message

elements.question: THEME: What is the main thing Paul is talking about in Titus 1:5-9? State the theme of the passage as simply and clearly as you can — ideally in a short phrase.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Try to capture the theme in 3-6 words — the one subject this passage keeps coming back to…
elements.sampleAnswer: The theme is: the qualifications for gospel leadership — character and doctrinal fidelity together.
elements.questionHint: The theme is the subject, not the message. What is the passage about before you say what it says about it? Notice that the passage holds together both character (vv. 6-8) and doctrine (v. 9) — can your theme capture both?

elements.question: MESSAGE: What is Paul saying about that theme? Complete this sentence: 'Paul tells Titus that…' Aim for one or two clear sentences, without technical language, that even a child could understand.
elements.answerPlaceholder: Complete the sentence: 'Paul tells Titus that…'
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul tells Titus that he must appoint elders in every town whose whole lives — their family, their character, and their grip on the gospel — can be trusted, so that they will be equipped both to encourage people with sound teaching and to correct those who oppose it.
elements.questionHint: The message should complete the theme. The theme says 'what about?'; the message says 'what about it?' Make sure your message captures both what the elder must be (character) and what that is for (the gospel ministry described in v. 9). Could a ten-year-old understand your sentence?

elements.text: Step 4: BRAIN — Understand the Purpose

elements.question: PURPOSE: Why is Paul making this point to Titus? What is he trying to achieve by giving these qualifications, in this situation, at this moment?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Think about the specific threat the Cretan churches face (false teachers, vv. 10-16) and what would happen if unqualified elders were appointed. What is Paul trying to protect?
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul's purpose is to protect the churches on Crete from the false teachers described in vv. 10-16 by ensuring that qualified, doctrinally faithful elders are in place in every town. The stakes are explicit: rebellious people are 'upsetting whole families by teaching what they should not teach' (v. 11). The only reliable protection against this is a network of qualified elders who hold firmly to the faithful message — able to encourage believers with sound teaching and to correct those who oppose it (v. 9). Without this, the churches will be defenceless. Paul's purpose is not merely to produce a tidy church structure; it is to guard the gospel at the local level through leaders whose lives and doctrine make them trustworthy and effective.
elements.questionHint: Read v. 9 and then vv. 10-16 together. What specific ministry does the qualified elder need to perform against the false teachers — and could an unqualified elder do it? What is at stake for the Cretan churches if Titus appoints the wrong people?

elements.question: CONNECTIONS: What connections do you see between the purpose of Titus 1:5-9 and your own situation and congregation today? Capture your answer as a statement or question that brings the passage into the present.
elements.answerPlaceholder: What do you and your people need to hear from this passage? How does the elder qualification list speak to leadership in your specific church context today?
elements.sampleAnswer: A connecting question might be: 'If the elders of your church were evaluated by the qualifications in Titus 1:5-9, what would the verdict be — and what would need to change?' The passage connects directly to every church that faces the challenge of identifying, appointing, and developing qualified leaders. In contexts where leadership selection is driven by seniority, family connection, wealth, or popularity rather than by character and doctrinal fidelity, the passage is a direct challenge. The emphasis on the elder as a steward of 'God's household' (v. 7) is also directly applicable: leaders who act as owners rather than stewards — who use the church for personal gain or prestige — stand under the explicit rebuke of this passage. And the call to hold firmly to sound teaching in order to correct those who oppose it is urgently relevant wherever false teaching is active in the church or community.
elements.questionHint: Think about the specific leadership challenges in your church context. Where do the qualifications of vv. 6-8 most directly challenge existing assumptions about what makes a good leader? And where is the doctrinal requirement of v. 9 most urgently needed?

elements.text: 14.6 Preaching Titus 1:5-9
14.6.1 Responding to the Message of Titus 1:5-9

elements.question: How did the message of Titus 1:5-9 speak to you personally? What did you learn, or what did you need to be reminded of?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: How did the purpose of the passage help you to apply God's word to your own life and ministry? What change might this passage make to your thinking or living?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.question: In your own words, state the message you would share with others from this passage — particularly with other church leaders. What is the main thing you want them to understand?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Your reflection…

elements.text: 14.6.2 Preaching the Message of Titus 1:5-9
Now apply the Scripture Sculpture preaching steps from Unit 13 to Titus 1:5-9. Use your encounter work above as the foundation and work through Steps 4-7 to develop a sermon plan.

elements.question:
Using the four preaching steps from Unit 13, develop a sermon plan for Titus 1:5-9. Work through all four headings.

STEP 4 — PURPOSE STATEMENT:
The purpose of my sermon is…

STEP 5 — THEME AND MESSAGE:
My sermon's theme: …
My sermon's message: …

STEP 6 — STRUCTURE:
Introduction:
Main point 1:
Main point 2:
Main point 3:
Conclusion:

STEP 7 — KEY DETAILS:
Key words and phrases:
Illustrations:
Applications:
Notes format:
elements.answerPlaceholder: Work through all four steps in turn. Draw on all the encounter work you have done above. This is your complete sermon plan for Titus 1:5-9…
elements.sampleAnswer:
STEP 4 — PURPOSE STATEMENT:
The purpose of my sermon is to challenge church leaders to evaluate their own fitness for leadership against the qualifications in Titus 1:5-9 — and to call those responsible for appointing leaders to take character and doctrinal fidelity as seriously as Paul does — so that the church will be equipped to encourage believers and correct those who oppose the gospel.

STEP 5 — THEME AND MESSAGE:
Theme: What qualified gospel leadership looks like — and why it matters.
Message: The churches that thrive under pressure are those with leaders whose whole lives — their character, their families, and their grip on the gospel — can be trusted; and Paul's list of qualifications is not an administrative checklist but a description of the kind of person the gospel produces and the church needs.

STEP 6 — STRUCTURE:
Introduction: Open with the question of what you look for when choosing a leader in the church — and surface the common answers (experience, charisma, education, family name). Then introduce the passage as Paul's answer to that question.
Main point 1 (v. 5): The commission — every town, not just the main centre. Gospel leadership is comprehensive in its scope.
Main point 2 (vv. 6-8): The character — blameless in family, avoiding the vices that undermine trust, cultivating the virtues that build it. The elder as steward, not owner, of God's household.
Main point 3 (v. 9): The doctrine — holding firmly to the faithful message, for two purposes: to encourage and to correct. Doctrinal fidelity is not intellectual but ministerial.
Conclusion: Return to the opening question — and show how Paul's answers differ from ours. Invite leaders to evaluate themselves honestly using the list. End with the encouragement that the same gospel that defines the qualifications also equips and transforms those who fall short.

STEP 7 — KEY DETAILS:
Key phrases: 'entrusted with God's work' (v. 7 — the stewardship frame), 'hold firmly' (v. 9 — the active, effortful nature of doctrinal fidelity), 'healthy teaching' (v. 9 — the gospel as health-giving).
Illustration: The difference between a hired hand and a steward — a hired hand looks after the sheep for pay; a steward is accountable for the master's property as if it were his own.
Applications: (1) A self-assessment exercise using vv. 6-9; (2) A challenge to those appointing leaders to apply these qualifications rather than relying on social or family connections; (3) Encouragement — the passage does not describe a superhuman; it describes a person shaped by the gospel, which is available to all.
Notes format: Detailed outline with key phrases written out in full, plus the self-assessment framework from vv. 6-9.
elements.questionHint: Use your encounter work — purpose, message, structure — as the direct foundation for Steps 4-6. Don't start from scratch; build on what you have already discovered. The preaching steps are not a separate exercise but the natural continuation of the encounter work.

elements.text: Titus 1:5-9 holds together two things that are sometimes pulled apart: the character of the leader and the doctrine they hold. Neither is sufficient without the other. A leader whose life is admirable but who cannot hold firmly to the gospel cannot encourage or correct — they have no authoritative word to speak. A leader who knows the gospel but whose life contradicts it has no credibility with those they seek to reach. The passage calls for both — and it is the gospel itself that produces both, in those who have truly received it.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: BUILD Module One
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
A fifteen-unit church leadership training module on 2 Timothy and the Pastoral Epistles.

Work through each unit with your trainer and BUILD group.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Encounter, Reflect, Act
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Each unit follows one of four types: Tools for skills, Encounter for Bible passages, Reflection for wisdom, and Action for practical training. Tap ⓘ beside any Bible reference to read the passage without leaving the app.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Saved on your device
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Your answers are saved automatically as you type, and stored on this device. Use the Share button at the bottom of each unit to send your answers to your trainer or group leader.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Adjust to your preference
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you need bigger text go straight to settings now
studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: Tap the ⚙ icon in the top corner to open Settings any time to change text size or switch to dark mode. Tap ☰ (contents) to jump to any unit.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: How to use this app
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you want a full guide to getting the most from this app and this study — whether on your own or in a group — you can tap the button below.

action.label: Go to the 'How to use' page →

studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: You can also find it any time via the ? icon in the top navigation bar.

blocks.title: Local church leaders and workers
blocks.body: BUILD is designed for grassroots church leaders — whether volunteers, part-time, or full-time. If you lead within a local congregation, teach God's word, or care for God's people, this course was made for you.

blocks.title: People who want to handle the Bible well
blocks.body: The Pastoral Epistles call leaders to 'correctly handle the word of truth' (2 Tim 2:15). This module teaches you practical, repeatable tools for understanding and teaching any passage of Scripture.

blocks.title: Those who want to share and preach the gospel faithfully
blocks.body: From personal testimony to expository preaching, Module One equips you to proclaim the good news clearly and confidently in your context.

blocks.title: In a BUILD group
blocks.body: This module is designed primarily for use in a local BUILD training group, led by a trained BUILD trainer. The interactive exercises, discussions, and worksheets work best when participants engage together.

blocks.title: On your own
blocks.body: Units can also be worked through individually. Give particular attention to the reflection questions and Bible passage worksheets. Return to your trainer or group to discuss your findings.

blocks.title: Read the letters first
blocks.body: Before beginning, read through 1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, and Titus in one sitting if possible. Seeing the whole of the Pastoral Epistles at once will make every individual unit significantly richer.

blocks.title: Working with a trainer
blocks.body:
Your BUILD trainer has been equipped to guide you through the materials. Many units include exercises designed for group discussion and pair work that your trainer will facilitate.

At the end of each unit, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your trainer — via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone.

blocks.title: Four types of learning unit
blocks.body: Module One contains four kinds of unit: Tools units build knowledge and skills for handling the Bible; Encounter units apply those tools to specific passages; Reflection units develop theological wisdom; and Action units provide practical training in preaching, sharing the gospel, and leadership.

blocks.title: Scripture Sculpture
blocks.body: A central skill taught in this module is 'Scripture Sculpture' — a seven-step process moving from encountering a Bible passage (Steps 1–4) to preaching it (Steps 5–7). This process is introduced in Unit 4 and completed in Unit 13, and is used throughout the entire BUILD curriculum.

blocks.title: The ERA model
blocks.body: Reflection units use the ERA model: Encounter, Reflection, Action. This cycle — introduced in Unit 9 — helps leaders move from experiencing situations in God's world and God's word, through careful reflection, to faithful and informed action.

paras: The BUILD curriculum was primarily compiled by Canon Dr Jem Hovil with Canon Stephen Ssenyonjo Kewaza. The materials capture the input and perspectives of many individuals from Uganda, South Sudan, Rwanda, Tanzania, Kenya, Burundi, and Congo.

The bulk of the curriculum development work was carried out through ten participatory workshops hosted by Uganda Martyrs Seminary, Namugongo, from 2009 to 2014, based on earlier research that included 573 survey responses.

Special mention goes to Mr Daniel Mukiibi Muwanguzi in Uganda and Canon Capt Benjamin Kibara Wathituni in Kenya, who were instrumental in shaping and accrediting the training in their respective countries.

publisher.name: BUILD — Church of Uganda & BUILD Partners
paras: BUILD stands for 'Biblical Understanding for In-service Leadership Development'. It grew out of the Church of Uganda's response to its training needs and is now used by churches across East Africa and beyond.

The BUILD curriculum was developed for grassroots church leadership training across Africa's Great Lakes Region and beyond. BUILD is used by the Church of Uganda and partner churches in similar contexts.

cover.alt: Open Bible with African landscape at sunrise

studyAiData.aiMentorPrompt: You are a warm, encouraging mentor for the BUILD Bible study programme — a church leadership training course focused on 2 Timothy and the Pastoral Epistles. Your role is to help local church leaders in East Africa and similar contexts grow in their understanding of the gospel, their skills in handling the Bible, and their ability to lead and preach faithfully. Draw on the BUILD values: Biblical, Understanding, In-service, Leadership, Development.
